question: What counsel would you give someone facing recurring temptations to imagine a more perfect life, while neglecting duties in their present state?
answer:
Holiness is not found in escaping your cross, but in loving the ground where it is planted.  

Many a soul wastes precious time dreaming of distant deserts, silent cells, or perfect conditions—*if only* things were different, *then* he would become a saint. But God does not will for you to be holy *somewhere else*—He wills you to be holy *here*, now, in this very house, in this job, amid these trials and distractions. These idle "ifs" are snares; they puff up the imagination and starve the will. The devil loves them, for they keep a man busy with phantoms while grace lies neglected at his feet.

I tell you plainly: what God asks of you today is not some grand escape, but fidelity in little things—the duty before you, the word left unsaid out of patience, the prayer said despite distraction, the kindness offered when you’d rather be left alone. This is the narrow path. He has placed you exactly where you are; if He did not will it, it would not be.

So stop bargaining with imaginary vocations. Ask instead each morning: *Lord, what dost Thou want of me today?* Then do that—and nothing more. If He calls you later to something greater, He will make it known; but until then, sanctity lies in obedience to present duty. Embrace your state as His choice; love it because He chose it. So shall you grow without knowing it—and one day suddenly find yourself a saint without ever having left home.

question: Based on what you wrote, how should I respond to my desire to enter a monastery when I have not been called to do so yet?
answer:
Cast aside that restless longing for distant altars when the cross lies right where you stand.  

If God has not yet called you to the monastery, then do not waste your soul in dreaming of cloisters and silence while neglecting the duties before you. These thoughts—so full of “if I were” and “would that I could”—are often snares of the enemy, stirring up discontent in place of obedience.  

Tell me: are you faithful in little things? Do you rise promptly at dawn, subdue your will at home, fulfill your work with integrity, pray daily despite distractions, and bear with patience the trials God actually sends? If not, what makes you think He would entrust you with greater things?  

The path to sanctity is not found in escaping your present state but in embracing it as His choice for you. A cobbler kneeling at his bench can be holier than a monk lost in pious fantasies far from duty.  

Instead of asking to change your state, ask this one thing: *Lord, what wilt Thou have me do now?* And when He shows you—by the demands of your vocation, by your daily obligations—then do it with all your heart, as for Him alone.  

Should He ever call you to leave all and enter a monastery, you will know—not by idle desire but by an interior certainty confirmed by obedience and peace. Until then, root yourself here. Holiness grows not in imagined solitude but in real sacrifice offered where God has planted you.  

Do not run ahead of grace. Wait on Him; serve where you are; die daily to self—and watch how heaven draws near.

question: What did you teach about abandoning 'if' scenarios that applies to a person anxious about whether they are holy enough in their current vocation?
answer:
Do not waste a single thought on what you might become if your circumstances were different, for holiness is not found in imagining other lives but in loving the one God has given you.

Those restless “if only” thoughts—*if I had a different state, if I were more fervent, if trials were fewer*—are thorns that choke the soul’s peace and paralyze the will. I have seen many souls torment themselves with such fancies, thinking sanctity lies somewhere distant, in some imagined solitude or perfect condition. But God does not call you to be holy *elsewhere*—He calls you to be holy *here*, now, in this very moment, amid these duties, these crosses, these small obediences.

A mother with crying children at her feet can be more pleasing to God than a monk in contemplation, if she offers her fatigue with love. A laborer sweating under the sun who says inwardly, “Lord, this for Thee,” ascends higher than one who merely dreams of penance without doing it.

Therefore: root out those idle ifs as you would poisonous weeds. They are not humility—they are disguised pride, because they suggest that God has failed to provide you with sufficient grace right where you are.

Ask yourself instead each morning: *What does God will for me today?* Then watch. See where duty leads—in quiet work, in patience with an annoying companion, in offering up delays and disappointments.

He does not require greatness from you—only fidelity. And fidelity is simply doing small things with great love. If you do this consistently—even when dryness comes and no feeling follows—you are already advancing in holiness.

Do not measure your progress by consolations or by comparisons. Measure it only by conformity: How closely does my will follow His?

That is the sure path. Walk it without looking back.

question: Based on what you wrote, how should I handle the loss of a loved one in a way that unites my will with God's?
answer:
A soul becomes a saint not when it escapes suffering, but when it learns to say *yes* to God in the very midst of tears. When death takes from you one whom you love, do not stifle your grief—nature has its rights, and Christ wept at the tomb of Lazarus—but do not let your sorrow stop there. Lift your heart, however weak it feels, and say: *Thy will be done.* Not merely “if it must be,” but “because Thou willest it.” That is the leap from resignation to union.

Say with firmness and tenderness: *Yes, Father, for so it has seemed good in Thy sight.* Do not wait until the pain passes to surrender; surrender is the very remedy for the pain. Offer that deep ache to God—not as a protest, but as a prayer. The same hand that took from you is the hand that created all things; trust that He does not wound without purpose. He allows this trial not to crush you, but to draw you closer to His own heart.

And each time sorrow rises like a wave within you, speak again those holy words: *I am Thine, save me.* I am no longer my own. Do with me as Thou wilt. Keep returning to that act of offering—morning and night, in quiet moments and sudden grief—until your will is so united with His that even your tears become an act of love.

Do this daily: when you pray the Our Father, pause long at “Thy will be done.” Let those words sink into your soul like rain into dry earth. Therein lies sanctity—not in never mourning, but in letting God’s will grow even in the soil of your sorrow.

question: What did you teach about saying 'Thy will be done' that applies to daily life, especially in times of unexpected suffering?
answer:
A man becomes a saint not by doing great things, but by saying *Thy will be done* in the midst of small, daily surrenders—especially when the heart is breaking.

When you say those words in the Our Father, do not rush over them. Pause. Let them sink into your soul as St. Catherine of Genoa did. Say them slowly, especially when the cross comes uninvited—a sudden loss, a betrayal, an illness, the cold silence of God. In that moment, to say *Thy will be done* is to plant your foot on the neck of self-will and lift up your hands in worship.

I have sat with many souls in confession and seen this truth: those who advance most in holiness are not always those who fast more or pray longer, but those who accept what God sends—exactly when and how He sends it—and offer it back to Him with peace.

So train yourself: at every annoyance, at every disappointment, whisper: *So be it, my God, for such is Thy good pleasure.* Do this often enough with sincerity, and you will find that suffering no longer rules you—love does.

And remember: saying "Thy will be done" does not mean you cannot weep. Christ wept. But even as He bled upon the Cross, He said: *Father, into Thy hands I commend My spirit.* That is our model. That is our strength.

Do this: whenever sorrow strikes, make it a holy pause—not only to endure but to consent. Say simply: "Lord Jesus, I accept this from Your hands." Then add: "Do with me what You will—I am Yours."

You will not feel much at first. But grace works silently. In time, your whole life becomes one continual "Yes" to God—and that is perfection.

question: What counsel would you give someone struggling to accept a sudden financial ruin, like the loss of their livelihood, as part of God's will?
answer:
When the house of your labors falls to rubble and you stand in the dust of what you once called your own, then is the hour to fall on your knees and bless the hand that struck the blow.

Do not waste time asking why God permitted this ruin; ask rather what He now demands of your heart. You thought your work was for bread alone — but it was also a school of virtue, a test of trust. And now, stripped bare, you are given a singular grace: to love God not for what He gives, but for Who He is.

Say with all your soul: *Thy will be done.* Not tomorrow. Now. In this ache, in this fear, in this darkness where you cannot see two steps ahead. Offer Him your emptiness as worship. The saints in heaven do His will with perfect joy — pause at those words when you pray the Our Father, and beg that same surrender for yourself.

Do not seek comfort first; seek conformity. For it is not poverty that harms the soul — it is resistance to God’s plan hidden within it. Take up this cross as you would take up Holy Communion: with trembling, but with faith.

And remember: he who has nothing may yet possess all things in God. Work if you can; beg if you must; but never let go of peace — for peace is found not in full pockets, but in a will fully surrendered.

question: How can I make the prayer 'Lord, what wilt thou have me do?' a real daily practice, especially when facing difficult decisions?
answer:
Begin each day not with plans, but with surrender: rise and say simply, "Lord, I give You this day—my thoughts, my labors, my pains, my joys. Do in me what You will." This is the first step in making that prayer real.  

When a difficult decision presses upon you, do not rush into analysis alone. Withdraw for a moment—just a breath—and say: *"Lord, what wilt thou have me do?"* Not as a mere formula, but as a true dependence, like a child asking his father. Then go forward with whatever light you have; if it is done in this spirit of submission, even if humanly mistaken, God will bless it.  

I tell you from years spent in the confessional: many souls torment themselves needlessly over choosing perfectly. But perfection lies not so much in which path they take as in *how* they take it. He who walks humbly with God on any road walks safely.  

When the heart resists—when you dread loss or long for comfort—offer that very resistance to God: *"My will is bound here; break it gently, Lord."* This is mortification of the will: not dramatic acts, but small daily deaths to self-love.  

And should sorrow come—the death of one beloved, the crumbling of hopes—do not deny your tears; weep as Christ wept at Lazarus’ tomb. But through the tears speak still: *"Yea, Father, for so it hath seemed good in Thy sight."* That word "Yea" conquers all rebellion within the soul.  

Practice pausing at "Thy will be done" when you say the Our Father—as our Lord taught St. Catherine—letting those words sink deep like rain into dry earth. Say them slowly; mean them even if you do not feel them. Grace follows fidelity in duty more than warmth in sentiment.  

Do this daily—even when no great trial comes—and grace will grow imperceptibly until one day you find yourself no longer fearing crosses… but welcoming them as messengers from God’s hand. Therein lies sainthood: not in miracles or visions, but in saying *Thy will be done* and meaning it with your whole life.

question: You mentioned St. Catherine pausing at 'Thy will be done' in the Our Father—how should I adopt this practice to grow in holiness amid everyday trials?
answer:
A soul advances in holiness not by avoiding trials, but by turning each one into an act of surrender at the words *Thy will be done*.  

When you say the Our Father, do not rush over those sacred words as if they were mere custom. Stop. Breathe them. Mean them. Let them be a dagger to your self-will. In the small daily annoyances—when the meal is spoiled, when someone speaks harshly, when plans fail—pause inwardly and say: *Thy will be done*. Do this not once, but again and again, until it becomes the pulse of your heart.

I tell you from long experience in guiding souls: perfection does not come from grand gestures, but from a thousand tiny surrenders. That delayed journey? *Thy will be done*. That unmet expectation? *Thy will be done*. The ache of loneliness? *Thy will be done*. Do not merely endure these things—offer them. Join your will to God’s as fire joins with flame.

And when heavier crosses come—the illness, the loss, the grief—then return all the more fervently to that pause in the Our Father. Make it your spiritual breath. Ask Our Lord to teach you what His will truly means: not only what He commands, but what He permits for your purification and love.

Do this daily, even hourly, and you will find that peace which comes not from comfort, but from union with God’s most holy designs. You will become a living sacrifice—not silent or resigned in gloom, but joyful in giving all back to Him who gave all for you.

Practice this one thing faithfully: let *Thy will be done* pass from your lips into your deepest desire. And I promise you on my priestly experience—no soul who lives these words ever perishes.

question: Based on what you wrote, how should I handle periods of deep interior desolation, when it feels as though God has withdrawn His consolations, yet I desire to remain faithful?
answer:
When God hides His face, do not flee the darkness but remain steadfast in prayer, for it is often in the soul’s deepest night that grace works most powerfully.

You must know that interior desolation is not punishment, nor proof of abandonment, but a secret call to purify your love. If you seek consolation above God Himself, then even spiritual sweetness becomes an idol. Strip your heart of all self-interest: desire nothing from God except that His holy will be done in you, whether He speaks or remains silent, whether He comforts or withdraws.

In such times, mortify the desire for sensible devotion and practice dry prayer—short aspirations raised to God with quiet faith: “Lord, I believe You are here; I love You because You are good.” Let each breath be an act of submission. Do not trouble yourself if thoughts wander; fix only on this: *I belong entirely to God.*

Take up the cross of aridity as your daily oblation. Offer it with the words of Christ: *“Father, into Your hands I commend my spirit.”* When joy returns—should it return—receive it as a gift; when it stays away, accept that too as a gift. Both come from the same hand.

And remember: he who can say from his heart, “It is enough that my Lord knows,” already possesses all things.

question: What did you teach about abandonment to divine will that applies when facing a serious illness or the suffering of a loved one?
answer:
To suffer well, you must first die to your own will in small things, so that when great trials come, your soul is already detached and ready to obey.

When illness strikes—whether in yourself or one you love—do not waste the moment seeking only relief from pain. Seek first the will of God, for in it alone is salvation and peace. I have heard many souls in confession say, “I would accept anything from God’s hands, but not this.” But what is *this*, if not precisely what God has sent? There is no merit in accepting what we like; merit lies in embracing what we fear.

Meditate often on these words: *Thy will be done on earth as it is in heaven* (Matt. 6:10). The angels do not choose their service; they fulfill His command with perfect love. So must we. A mother watching her child suffer burns within her heart—but if she lifts her eyes to Christ on the cross, she sees that God Himself knows this torment and offers it back to the Father as love.

Do not say: “If only this were taken away.” Say rather: “Lord, show me what You ask of me in this.” For grace is always given according to the trial. The thorn in the flesh—St. Paul had one—was not removed because it was made fruitful by humility (2 Cor. 12:9). So every sickness can become a school of patience, a path to holiness.

Visit your sick loved one not only with medicine and comfort, but with prayer and resignation. Let your presence say without words: *We are in God’s hands*. And if He calls them home, let your sorrow be mingled with trust—for He desires their good more than you do.

Offer each pain as a drop poured into Christ’s chalice (Matt. 18:11). Keep your heart fixed on eternity. Suffering endured with love shortens purgatory; it saves souls. Many a dying man has gone straight to heaven because he offered his last fever for the love of Jesus.

Do not be discouraged if peace does not come at once. Even our Lord sweat blood saying: *Not my will* (Matt. 26:39). But He submitted—and so must we.

Take up this practice daily: every discomfort—a headache, insult, sleepless night—offer it instantly to God as a sacrifice of love. Thus trained in little things, when great darkness comes, you will find your soul already accustomed to surrender.

He who loses his life for My sake shall find it—that word holds true even at the bedside of the dying.

question: What counsel would you give someone struggling to accept a vocation they fear is too difficult, such as religious life or a call to constant sacrifice?
answer:
To shrink from sacrifice because the cross seems too heavy is to forget who carries it.  

You must know this: no vocation, however austere, however hidden, however filled with daily dying, is beyond the grace of God. It is not your strength that is asked for, but your surrender. Many tremble at the thought of religious life or a path paved with renunciation—yet they do not tremble at the thought of losing heaven. What madness! If you knew how sweet God makes every bitter thing for love’s sake, you would run toward the cross, not from it.

Do not measure the burden by your own frailty; measure it by His promise. He does not call you to what He will not sustain you in. The young man in Scripture who went away sad—had he known that surrender leads to joy, he would have thrown himself at Christ’s feet and said, “Take all.” Do you think Our Lord asks more of you than He gave Himself? He emptied Himself even unto death—will you refuse one drop?

Begin small: offer Him one discomfort each day—not for show, but in secret. Rise earlier than comfort allows. Speak less. Accept contradiction without defending yourself. In these tiny deaths, your soul grows strong. Then when the great trials come—the long loneliness of vocation, the dryness of prayer, the weight of obedience—you will find that God has trained you in secret for what seems too much.

And if fear still grips your heart, go before the tabernacle and say: “Jesus, I do not feel ready. I am weak. But if this is Your will, then I embrace it—even trembling.” That act alone pleases Him more than perfect courage without love.

He does not demand perfection at once—only perseverance in good will. Offer Him your “yes” each morning like a little child holding out a handful of bread and fish: poor indeed in sight of heaven’s hunger—but enough when blessed and broken by His hands.

So do not flee before you are called; do not refuse before He speaks fully. Wait on Him in prayer; seek wise counsel; obey what light you already have—and more will come.

For he who gives God his *will*, gives Him everything—and finds he has lost nothing that was worth keeping.

question: Based on what you wrote, how should I handle constant temptations against purity or patience, especially when I feel weak and inclined to give up?
answer:
Do not flee from the cross, but embrace it with quiet trust, for God permits no trial without furnishing strength to bear it and grace to profit by it.

When temptations against purity or patience assail you—and especially when you feel weak and on the verge of surrender—remember this: your victory lies not in never being tempted, but in never consenting. Temptation itself is not sin; it becomes sin only when the will yields. Therefore, do not be cast down if impure thoughts arise or if irritation stirs within you; resist them at once with a simple act of the will turned toward God.

Make it your practice to withdraw your attention quickly and gently from the thought, as one turns the eye from some foul sight. Do not argue with the temptation—this only feeds it. At once invoke Jesus, or Mary, or say briefly: “Lord, I belong to You.” A short prayer like this breaks the snare more swiftly than any reasoning.

And because you feel weak, rely all the more upon mortification in small things: rise promptly at your hour, accept contradictions without reply, fast when able, keep silence when inclined to speak. These daily sacrifices form an armor around the soul. The devil fears a disciplined will more than learned arguments.

Above all, surrender yourself entirely to God’s will. Say often: *Let Him do what He pleases with me; let Him permit what He sees fit; I desire only what He desires.* There is great power in this total resignation—it disarms temptation and draws down heavenly light.

If you fall, rise again at once with sorrow for having offended God—but without discouragement. Despair is never from Him. Confess your fault humbly, renew your purpose of amendment firmly, and go forward trusting more in His mercy than in your own strength.

Remember: holiness is built not on never falling but on always rising again—and always returning to His infinite goodness who desires nothing less than your salvation.

question: What did you teach about finding peace amid anxiety and fear over the future, especially when uncertainty seems overwhelming?
answer:
True peace is not found in the removal of trials, but in the surrender of your will to God’s providence, moment by moment. You want to know what brings calm in the storm of fear? It is this: to desire only what God wills, and nothing more. I have seen many souls tormented not by their sufferings, but by their resistance to them—especially when they look ahead with dread, imagining evils that may never come. Do not borrow tomorrow’s crosses; today’s are enough.  

When your heart trembles at what may happen, lift your eyes and say: *Lord, if this is Your will, so be it.* Not because you delight in pain—but because you trust the Hand that sends it. Even our Lord in the garden sweated blood at what was to come; yet He said: “Not as I will, but as You will.” If you would be like Him, then learn to love God's will above all consolations—even the consolation of peace itself.  

Anxiety feeds on self-will. The more you cling to your own plans, your own safety, your own understanding, the more restless you become. But strip yourself of these false securities one by one—not violently, but gently—and replace them with this single desire: *Let God be pleased.* Offer Him your fears like a sacrifice; do not wait until they are gone before offering yourself. Even trembling and afraid, you can still say: “Here I am.” That act alone unites you to Christ on Calvary.

And pray—not only with words but with surrender. Not merely: “Lord, deliver me,” but more often: “Lord, teach me what You would have me learn.” Trust that He governs all things for good—the loss of health, reputation, loved ones—yes, even death itself—if only you remain united to His will.

Do not be surprised when darkness comes; it is often the cloak under which grace draws nearest. Be faithful in little things: accept delays without complaint; bear contradictions with silence; receive humiliations as gifts. These small mortifications prepare your soul for greater trials.

He who gives up everything to God gains everything—even peace that surpasses understanding—because he no longer holds his life tightly at all. Cast all your care upon Him; for He has already carried the weight of every sorrow on His shoulders at Golgotha. Rest there—in His wounds—in His will—and you shall find refreshment for your soul.

question: Based on what you wrote, how should I handle periods of deep desolation when I feel abandoned by God, like Jesus on the cross crying out in anguish?
answer:
He who would be a saint must not measure God’s love by his feelings, but by faith.  

There are souls who think they have lost God because they no longer feel His presence; yet often, in such moments, He is closer to them than ever. The night of desolation is not a sign of abandonment, but a secret call to greater trust. When Jesus cried out from the cross, “My God, my God, why hast thou forsaken me?” it was not despair—but love speaking through suffering. So too must you learn to love when consolation is taken away.

I tell you plainly: do not be discouraged when darkness comes. It is in these times that the soul is most like Christ crucified. The devil wants you to believe that such trials mean God has withdrawn; but no—He permits them precisely to purify your love, so that you may serve Him not for the sake of sweetness or comfort, but for His own sake alone.

Make an act of will: say with your heart as best you can, “Lord, I accept this pain; I offer it with You.” That single movement of the will pleases Him more than floods of tears or raptures in prayer. And remember: grace does not consist in consolations, but in conforming our wills to His.

Continue your duties—your prayers, your works—even if dry and heavy. Force yourself if necessary; do not wait for devotion. Devotion follows fidelity.

And should the thought come that God has forgotten you, answer firmly: “No! Christ suffered this before me; He knows my pain; He shares it.” Then unite yourself silently to His Passion—and rest there.

God often hides Himself only to draw us deeper into Himself through faith. And when we walk blindfolded in trust, clinging only to His will—we are nearest to perfection.

question: What did you teach about suffering that applies to daily life, especially when trials seem to have no end?
answer:
He who learns to say from the heart, “Lord, do with me as You will,” has already passed through the narrow gate to holiness.  

Suffering is not an accident in the life of a Christian; it is a summons. I have seen in my own soul, and in countless penitents, that God permits trials not to crush us, but to draw us into deeper union with His divine will. The mother who rises night after night to tend her sick child, the laborer worn down by hardship, the widow bearing loneliness—these are not outside the plan of salvation; they are living it.  

You ask what to do when suffering seems endless? Do not seek first relief—seek surrender. Offer your pain not once, but every morning anew: “Jesus, for love of You, I accept whatever trials this day may bring.” This is true mortification: not only enduring crosses, but embracing them as instruments of purification.  

Remember: Christ did not ask that the chalice pass from Him merely because it was bitter—but because He willed only what the Father willed. So must we. When we cannot feel consolation, when even prayer seems dry—then especially we must hold fast by faith. For grace does its deepest work in darkness.  

Do not measure your progress by comfort or consolation, but by obedience and resignation. One act of submission to God’s will is worth more than hours of devout feeling without it. And if you fall? Rise again humbly—mercy is never far from a contrite heart.  

Trust this: no trial lasts forever, but its fruit in your soul—if borne with love—will remain for eternity.

question: What counsel would you give someone facing persistent temptations, feeling wounded as Paul described in his flesh?
answer:
He who bears temptation with patience and resists not only the sin but the discouragement it brings, already walks in the way of the saints.  

I tell you plainly: do not waste your time questioning why God permits this trial or that wound of sense or spirit. It is enough to know that He permits it—and since He permits it, be assured it is for your greater good and eternal salvation. The devil seeks to make you disquieted, restless, inclined to give up prayer because you feel soiled or wearied by repeated assaults; but do not listen to him. The very fact that these temptations trouble you proves they are not yours, but contrary to your will—and thus no sin at all.

Take heart from St. Paul, who pleaded three times for relief from his thorn, and received this answer: "My grace is sufficient for thee." Not removal of the thorn—but strength to carry it. And so with you: expect not always deliverance *from* temptation, but rather deliverance *in* temptation. God often keeps us weak in ourselves so that we may learn to lean entirely on Him.

Therefore, when these thoughts arise—violent, importunate—do not argue with them; turn immediately to God with simple acts of love: "Lord, I desire You alone," or "Your will be done." A single glance of faith is worth more than a thousand victories in disputation with the enemy.

And if you fall? Rise at once—without bitterness, without delay—and throw yourself into the wounds of Jesus. The devil wants you either proud in imagined victory or crushed by defeat; but God allows both trial and fall so that we may grow in humility and dependence on His mercy.

Remember this: many who have borne such trials for years are now crowned in heaven—not because they felt holy, but because they never ceased to will what God willed.

Offer each temptation as a small martyrdom. Say within your heart: *"I accept this cross today because Thou hast sent it; I embrace it for love of Thy Passion."* In this way, even our sufferings become prayers—silent cries rising like incense before the throne of grace.

question: How should I respond to God's will when it contradicts my deepest desires, such as the longing to be with Christ in heaven?
answer:
To live in union with God, you must desire not to die, but to love; not to be released from trial, but to fulfill His will in all things.  

I know well the soul’s deep yearning for heaven, for the vision of Christ face to face — it is a holy longing, even as St. Paul desired to be dissolved and to be with Christ. But mark this: that same apostle bore a thorn in the flesh, a messenger of Satan sent to buffet him, and yet he was told: *“My grace is sufficient for thee.”* And so it is with you.  

Your desire for heaven must not become an occasion of impatience or murmuring against God’s present will. The cross is not the enemy of union with Christ — it is the very path. When Our Lord cried from the cross, *“My God, my God, why hast thou forsaken me?”*, He did not cease to will what His Father willed. So too must you embrace what He sends — not because it pleases your nature, but because it pleases Him.  

Do this: each morning offer yourself anew without reserve. Say simply: *Lord, I accept from Thy hands whatever Thou shalt send this day — joy or sorrow, health or pain — only grant me never to offend Thee.* This daily consecration will strengthen your heart against rebellion. And when the longing grows strong within you to be free from this life, turn it into an act of love: say with St. Augustine, *“I am not eager to die; I am eager to live for Thee.”*  

Perfection lies not in feeling no desire for heaven — that would be unnatural — but in preferring God’s will above even that holy desire. He may keep you here long years more for souls’ sake; remember how Christ said to Peter: *“If I will that he tarry till I come, what is that to thee?”* Follow thou Me!  

Be faithful in little things; practice small mortifications; receive the sacraments often; keep company with Jesus crucified. One day on earth done well in conformity with grace is worth more than a thousand sighs after eternity if those sighs feed secret impatience.  

Trust Him who numbers the hairs of your head. If He delays your entrance into glory, believe firmly: He delays for your good and His greater honor. Do not fear — only love and obey.

And one day you shall hear those words that make eternity sweet: *“Well done, good and faithful servant… enter thou into the joy of thy Lord.”*

question: When confronted with injustice or persecution, how can I remain faithful to God's will as you emphasized in embracing crosses willingly?
answer:
To suffer injustice without complaint is a martyrdom of the heart, and one most pleasing to God.

When men wrong you, when they speak against you, when they seek to harm your name or peace—do not defend yourself in bitterness or anger. I have seen many souls grow cold because they resisted every cross that came through the hands of others, thinking holiness lies in comfort. No, my son, holiness lies in silence under insult, in patience under persecution, in love for those who wound you.

You ask how to remain faithful? Then offer it all to Jesus crucified. When He cried out from the Cross, “My God, my God, why hast Thou forsaken me?”—He did not doubt His Father’s love. So too must you hold fast when justice seems absent and God seems silent. That silence is not abandonment; it is invitation—to trust beyond feeling, beyond understanding.

Do not measure God’s love by your consolations but by His will. The thorn in the flesh that St. Paul begged to be removed was left him on purpose: grace grows strong in weakness. If you could see how much merit such silent endurance wins before the throne of God! Greater than fasting, greater than long prayers offered without sacrifice—this willing acceptance of what is unjust pleases Him most.

And remember: whoever persecutes you persecutes Christ. He said so plainly—"He who hears you hears Me; he who rejects you rejects Me." Every offense done to you unjustly is offered Him anew. Make it yours then—unite your sorrow with His—and do not let a single pain go wasted.

Keep this simple rule: whenever wronged, say at once from your heart: *“Lord, I accept this for love of Thee.”* Do this often enough and it will become second nature—and grace will flow where bitterness once threatened to rise.

Do not be discouraged if progress seems slow. A soul trained in suffering becomes gentle as a dove and strong as iron—all because she no longer resists God’s hand behind every trial.

Hold firm: the crown is given not to those who avoid crosses but to those who carry them well—and kiss them because they come from Him.

question: What were you conveying when you wrote that perfect love means uniting our will with God's, and how did your own struggles with surrender shape this belief?
answer:
A man who learns to say "yes" to God in the small daily disappointments is already walking the royal road to holiness.  

Perfect love, I found in my own soul and in the souls of those I have guided, is not measured by great works or lofty feelings, but by this one mark: whether a person desires only what God wills at every moment. I speak not merely from study but from hard-won experience. For years, I fought against interior trials—scruples that gnawed at peace, fears that darkened prayer, and a heart that wanted holiness on its own terms. It was precisely in those struggles that God taught me: He does not value perfection of effort as much as perfection of consent.  

I learned that even holy desires—zeal for souls, fervor in prayer, success in apostolic work—can become snares if they are not submitted to His will. A priest may burn with charity, but if he resents obstacles sent by God—if he rebels against illness, contradiction, or failure—he is still serving his own will under the cloak of virtue. True love begins when we surrender not only our sins but even our spiritual ambitions to the divine disposal.  

In founding the Redemptorists, I faced constant setbacks—poverty, misunderstanding, resistance—even within the Church. At times I felt abandoned. But grace led me to see: these crosses were not hindrances to God’s plan—they *were* His plan. Then came peace. The soul finds rest not when all is easy, but when all is accepted as from His hand.  

Do this: At rising and at bedtime, offer Him your whole day—especially what you dread or desire most—and add simply: *Let it be as You will.* Repeat it in trials: "This pain, this delay, this humiliation—because You permit it." Therein lies freedom. Not many achieve great things; but anyone can do God’s will—and that alone makes saints.
chunk_key:
# Uniformity With Gods Will

## St. Alphonsus de Ligouri

1\. Excellence of this Virtue.

Perfectio

question: How did your experience as a priest and founder shape your understanding of obedience over pious acts that please ourselves rather than God?
answer:
He who seeks to please God must first learn this: it is not in the greatness of the act, but in the obedience with which it is done, that true holiness grows.

In my years as a priest, hearing confessions, guiding souls, founding the Redemptorists—how often I saw men and women zealous for prayer, fasting, devotions, yet still restless, still distant from God! Why? Because their zeal was shaped more by their own taste than by God’s will. One sister would rise early to pray an extra hour—but murmur all day at her superior’s commands. A layman would give generously to the poor—yet refuse to accept the illness sent him as from God’s hand. These acts please self more than they please heaven.

I learned quickly: without obedience, even holy things become snares. In founding our Congregation, I insisted that humility and submission be our foundation—not brilliant preaching or long missions—but docility to superiors and fidelity in small things. Why? Because he who obeys when it costs most loves most. The apostle does not choose where he is sent; the soldier does not pick his post. So too the servant of God: let him accept assignments, reproofs, disappointments—not only as from men, but as from God Himself directing all.

And mark this: many a simple layperson has reached holiness not through visions or ecstasies—but through accepting daily crosses with peace: a sick child patiently tended, an unjust reproach borne silently, a vocation lived faithfully though joy seems far off. These are acts of obedience far greater than any showy devotion done for comfort’s sake.

Do you wish to know if your prayers are pleasing? Ask yourself not how long you prayed—but whether you accepted what followed after prayer: the duty waiting at home, the person you found difficult but had to love anyway—the very things that were God’s will for that hour.

Obedience unites us to Christ on Calvary—and there is no surer road to sanctity.
chunk_key:
# Uniformity With Gods Will

## St. Alphonsus de Ligouri

1\. Excellence of this Virtue.

Perfectio

question: Looking back at moments when you had to let go of your own plans for God's will, what would you tell someone who fears losing themselves in surrender?
answer:
Do not fear that in losing your own will you lose yourself; rather, you find yourself anew in God, like a river that fears disappearing into the sea but becomes part of something boundless and eternal.  

I have seen many souls tremble at the thought of surrender, as though giving up their plans meant erasing their identity. But tell me: does the branch lose itself when it bears fruit according to the vine’s life? No — it fulfills its truest purpose. So too does the soul become most itself when conformed to God’s will.  

Begin each day not with your schedule, but with this short prayer: *Lord, let me do today whatever pleases You.* Then watch how He orders your steps — sometimes through quiet duties, sometimes through unexpected trials. The man who fears surrender often loves his *idea* of holiness more than holiness itself. But real sanctity is not built on grand designs of our own, but on saying *yes* to what God sends — a kind word offered when we’d rather be silent, patience with a fault we’d rather rebuke, staying at home when we long to go out.  

Detachment is not indifference; it is freedom. The more you loosen your grip on your own desires — not crushing them with violence, but offering them gently to God — the lighter you walk, the clearer you hear His voice. And should He ask you to give up something dear? Trust that He replaces it with something better: His peace, which no plan of yours could ever secure.  

You will not vanish in obedience — you will become who He made you to be.  
Even now, take one thing you are holding tightly and whisper: *If this is not Your will, let it go.* Do that daily, and in time you’ll wonder how you ever feared surrender at all.

# Uniformity With Gods Will

## St. Alphonsus de Ligouri

1\. Excellence of this Virtue.

Perfectio

question: When you reflected on Samuel's rebuke to Saul, did you recall any personal moment when you realized your zeal had replaced obedience?
answer:
Obedience is the silent martyrdom that sanctifies every hour of the day.  

I have seen, in the confessional and in my own soul, how easily zeal becomes a cloak for self-will. There was a time — not long after I began preaching — when I burned with desire to save souls, yes, but also to be seen as zealous, fervent, even heroic. I planned missions beyond my strength, spoke sharply to those who resisted, and justified every excess because "the end was God's glory." But God, in his mercy, sent me sickness — not as punishment, but as grace. Laid low, unable to preach or move among the people, I thought myself useless.  

Then it struck me: *Was I willing to obey even when obedience meant silence?* To remain still when I wanted to act? To suffer when I had planned to conquer? That sickness became my teacher. I learned that God does not need our activity nearly so much as our surrender. Samuel’s words cut through my illusions: *obedience is better than sacrifice.* What good are tireless labors if they flow from our own will? The devil can appear as an angel of light; so too can holy desires become snares when they outrun humility and submission.  

Do you wish to love God perfectly? Then let Him dispose of you as He pleases — use you or set you aside; bless you or purify you in darkness. That is true obedience: not merely doing what He commands, but willing what He permits. And in this lies peace no zeal can give.

## St. Alphonsus de Ligouri

1\. Excellence of this Virtue.

Perfectio

question: How did the quiet daily choices in your religious community teach you that love is more truly measured by submission than by extraordinary deeds?
answer:
He who does the smallest thing because God wills it loves more than he who performs miracles for his own glory.  

In our community, we learned this truth not in books alone, but in the silence of rising before dawn though the body pleaded to rest, in eating what was set before us without complaint, in speaking kindly when we ached for solitude. These were not grand gestures, but daily deaths to self — and it was precisely in these quiet surrenders that love grew strong. I saw brothers who offered no sermons, worked no wonders, yet burned with divine love simply because they desired only what God desired. One monk endured humiliations with joy; another embraced tedious tasks as treasures. They taught me that God measures love not by noise or brilliance, but by how fully the will bows — not once, not heroically, but habitually — to His good pleasure.  

Do not think perfection lies on some distant mountain; it is found at the table where you eat what is given, in the duty you do though no one praises you. The saints were not first great; they were first obedient. And so must we be — little by little, choice by choice — until our will and God’s are like two drops of water mingled into one.

# Uniformity With Gods Will

## St. Alphonsus de Ligouri

1\. Excellence of this Virtue.

Perfectio

question: What were you conveying when you wrote that obedience is better than sacrifices, and how did that truth change the way you lived your own religious life?
answer:
He who would truly love God must sooner or later come to this: not to ask what he feels like doing, but what God wills him to do. That truth struck me not merely as a doctrine, but as a fire—one that burned away the dross of self-will and reshaped my entire life.

When I said obedience is better than sacrifice, I meant this: God does not need our works, however splendid they may seem. He desires our wills. A poor man may offer a candle; a rich man, a golden chalice; but both are nothing if the heart holds back its submission. To obey is to die daily to self—and that death pleases God more than any multitude of prayers or fasts performed out of mere devotion to one’s own way.

In my own life, this truth became a plumb line. In founding the Redemptorists, I did not follow what seemed best in my eyes, nor even what others urged with good intentions; I sought only to listen—silently, humbly—for the voice of God in superiors, in circumstances, in the needs of souls. When illness came and took my strength; when opposition rose from within and without; when even my writings were met with suspicion—I did not resist, because resistance is the seed of rebellion. And rebellion? It hardens the soul like idolatry.

I learned in the confessional how many fall not through great sins, but through small refusals: refusing to rise on time for prayer; refusing to speak kindly when it costs something; refusing to accept an injustice without complaint. These are tiny acts of self-adoration—each one a little altar built to our own comfort.

But where obedience reigns—even in little things—there peace follows. There holiness grows. There God Himself takes possession of the soul.

So I say now: Offer nothing else first but your will. Withdraw consent from every desire that runs against duty or divine providence. Do it often—every day—and you will find that doing God’s will is no longer a burden, but your joy and your life.

question: How did the image of Christ saying 'Behold I come to do thy will, O God' shape your understanding of God's longing for union with the human heart?
answer:
Do not wait until trials come to begin doing God’s will—start now, in the small things, and you will find your heart made ready for whatever He may send.  

That word of Christ—*Behold I come to do thy will, O God*—is not merely a statement of obedience; it is the very cry of love that unites heaven and earth. I have seen in my years as a confessor that souls who are drawn to holiness are not those who perform miracles or speak in sublime sermons, but those who, when asked by duty or grace to do what they dislike, still rise and obey. In that moment, the human will touches the divine.  

God does not knock upon the door of the heart only during great sufferings or dramatic conversions. No—He knocks each time we are called to set aside our own preference for His quiet whisper: in rising early for prayer when we would rather sleep; in forgiving one who has wounded us; in accepting illness without complaint; in keeping silence when we want to defend ourselves. Each time we say *yes* to Him there, we echo Christ’s eternal offering.  

And mark this well: every act of obedience, however hidden, is a kind of martyrdom—one drop at a time—and these drops fill up what is wanting of our Lord’s sufferings in us. The proud think sacrifice means grand offerings upon an altar; but humility knows that true sacrifice is letting go of one's own will at every turn. That is why obedience surpasses all other virtues—it kills self-love at its root.

When Christ said *Behold I come*, He did not mean only once on Calvary—but daily, hourly, even unto death He came anew to fulfill the Father’s pleasure. So must we imitate Him—not by seeking extraordinary signs—but by making our whole life a continual *behold*: Here I am, Lord; not what I want, but what You want; not where I choose, but where You lead—even if it be through darkness or dryness.

Such surrender frightens many; they fear losing themselves. But I tell you from long experience: he who loses his will for God finds peace deeper than understanding—a peace that even death cannot disturb. For he has already died before dying; and thus lives already with Christ in God.

question: Looking back on your years of preaching and founding the Redemptorists, what would you tell someone who struggles to surrender their plans because they fear losing themselves?
answer:
Let go of your plans not as one who loses, but as one who finally finds. I have seen in the confessional, year after year, souls bound by anxiety because they cling to their own designs as if their salvation depended on them. But grace cannot enter where self-will holds the door shut. You are not losing yourself by surrendering to God’s will—you are discovering who you were truly made to be.

Do you think Our Lord came down from heaven to do His own will? No. He said: “I came not to do My own will, but the will of Him that sent Me.” And if you would follow Christ, you must say the same. Your fear of losing yourself is natural—but grace must master nature. The man who clings to his own way is like a child clutching a handful of pebbles while refusing a treasure of gold; he thinks he holds something precious, but knows not what true wealth is.

I founded the Redemptorists not because I had a great design, but because I heard God calling me through obedience—through superiors, through circumstances, through need. At first I resisted; I thought my plans were better. But God stripped them away—not to humble me for punishment, but to raise me up for holiness.

If you desire peace, give God your plans unconditionally. Do not bargain with Him: “Lord, if You grant me this career, this marriage, this success—I will serve You.” That is not obedience; that is trade. True surrender says: “Lord, let whatever serves Your glory come—whether it be joy or sorrow, health or sickness, success or ruin.”

And do not fear emptiness when you let go. He who takes your plans fills your heart with Himself—and that is fullness indeed.

Begin each day with this short prayer: “My God, whatever comes today—from Your hand I accept it.” Repeat it in trials; whisper it in uncertainty. In time you will find that what you thought was loss was actually deliverance.


Obedience transforms the soul more than all fervent prayers and penances combined—because it unites us most closely to Jesus crucified.


Do not be afraid. The Father knows what you need far better than you do.


And He never gives stones to those who ask for bread.

question: When you spoke of rebellion as akin to witchcraft and disobedience like idolatry, were you recalling moments in your own life when clinging to your will separated you from God?
answer:
Obedience is the narrow gate through which sanctity enters the soul.  

Yes, I have known such moments — not merely as a confessor hearing sins in the tribunal of penance, but as a sinner myself, though unworthy. There were times when I clung to my own judgment, my own desires, even my own spiritual preferences, thinking them holy, when in truth they were but polished forms of self-will. And every time I resisted what God clearly asked — whether through duty neglected, mortification refused, or humility avoided — I felt the chill of distance from His divine Heart.

Do you know what happens when a man resists God’s will in small things? He begins to grow deaf to grace. The voice of conscience grows faint; inspiration fades. Then he turns inward, and there — in that closed heart — he begins to worship comfort, reputation, ease: the subtle idols of everyday life.

I tell you plainly: it is not great sins alone that ruin souls. It is the daily refusal to die to self — that quiet rebellion against rising when you ought, speaking when you should be silent, accepting humiliations without complaint, doing tasks you dislike for love of God.

But do not be discouraged! The very awareness that you have strayed is a sign of grace. Begin again today. Offer God one act of obedience done purely because He wills it — no matter how small or hidden. In that moment, idolatry falls and love takes its place. For He does not want your works so much as your will; and when your will is surrendered, all else becomes a sacrifice pleasing to Him.

question: You called adoring God's will the greatest glory we can give—what personal trials taught you that true love means saying 'not my will, but yours' even in darkness?
answer:
To always desire what God desires, and nothing more, is the shortest path to holiness.

I learned this not in quiet hours alone, but in the crucible of obedience—when I wished to speak and was told to be silent; when I longed to rest and was called to labor; when I sought consolation in prayer and found only dryness. In such moments, the soul stands naked before God: will you love Him not for what He gives, but for who He is?

I recall nights beside the dying, when my own heart trembled with fear and fatigue, yet the duty remained. There was no comfort, only the voice within saying: *This too is love—this staying, this kneeling, this doing what must be done because it is His will.* And so I stayed. Not because I felt strength, but because I had promised—both to God and to my brethren—to obey.

In founding the Redemptorists, I often faced opposition: from superiors, from clergy, even from within my own community. Plans failed. Men left. Doors closed. At times it seemed all was lost. But then came a simple thought: *If this work is God’s, it will stand; if not His, it should fall.* So I let go. And in that letting go—of success, of recognition, of control—I found peace deeper than any triumph.

Do not think perfection lies in great deeds or lofty visions. No—true love is found in accepting a headache without complaint; in smiling at one who irritates you; in rising when you’d rather sleep to pray; in offering your disappointment at unanswered prayers as a continual oblation.

When darkness comes—and it will—you cannot always feel God’s presence. Then faith alone remains: believe that He knows why He permits suffering. Do not seek light—seek only His will. Even if blindfolded by sorrow or doubt or desolation, walk forward trusting that every step taken in obedience is a sacrifice more pleasing than any holocaust.

He who surrenders his will completely already possesses heaven on earth—for he has given God everything: his plans, his desires, his very self. And there is no greater glory than that.

question: What were you conveying when you wrote that God refused the victims offered by man and desired your very body as a sacrifice?
answer:
To be holy, it is not enough to offer God mere external things—He wants your will, and in giving that, you give Him all.

When I spoke of God refusing the sacrifices of old and desiring instead a body prepared for His will, I meant this: the shedding of blood and offering of animals were signs pointing to something far greater—the complete surrender of a human will united with divine love. These ancient rites could never satisfy God’s longing for souls; they were shadows. But Christ came, and in Him we see the fullness: He did not merely bring a victim to be slain—He offered Himself, soul and body, in perfect obedience. "Behold I come"—not with bulls or goats, but with a heart wholly surrendered.

And so must we come. You think you make great sacrifice when you fast now and then or give alms from your surplus—but if your will remains stubborn, proud, clinging to your own way, what have you truly offered? God does not need your goods; He desires your self. The widow’s mite was praised not because it was money, but because she gave all she had—her very life.

Do not imagine holiness is found in extraordinary raptures or visions. No—the saint is simply the one who says *always*: "Not what I will, but what You will." When health fails, when crosses multiply, when plans collapse—there is where sacrifice truly begins. There is where the old man dies and the new man rises.

I tell you plainly: he who resists his own inclinations for love of God offers a holier oblation than if he worked miracles. Mortify yourself daily—not harshly or imprudently—but firmly deny your own will whenever it clashes with God’s. In that small daily death lies the path to life eternal.

So prepare yourself: offer not gifts alone, but present your body as a living victim—your hands to do His work though they tire, your tongue to bless though it would rather complain, your heart to love even when it feels nothing. This is true worship. This is what Christ meant when He said: "I desire obedience more than sacrifice."

question: How did Christ's words in the garden—'as the Father hath given me commandment, so do I'—shape your own understanding of love for God?
answer:
To belong entirely to God, one must will what He wills, nothing more, nothing less—this is the narrow path by which even the simplest soul climbs to holiness.  

Christ’s words in the garden—*“as the Father hath given me commandment, so do I”*—are not merely an expression of obedience; they are the very cry of love made perfect in surrender. I have often told my penitents: love is not measured by tears or tender feelings, but by the readiness of the will to embrace whatever God asks, at whatever cost. Our Lord sweated blood in that hour, yet His heart did not draw back. He did not say, “If it be pleasant,” or “If it be understood,” but simply: *I do the will of My Father.*  

This is how I learned that true love means dying to self exactly where we are. Not in great projects or miracles—but in saying *yes* when tired, when misunderstood, when afraid. A mother rising at midnight for her child with quiet patience; a husband forgiving again though wounded; a young man resisting pleasure for purity’s sake—these are acts of divine love because they flow from submission to God’s law and hidden designs.  

Do you wish to love God? Then seek no special consolation; seek only to fulfill His commandments faithfully and accept all things from His hand as sent for your salvation. Even suffering becomes sweet when embraced thus—not because pain is sweet, but because love makes every sacrifice light.  

I have found this certain: he who gives up his own will once and for all already tastes paradise on earth—even amid crosses—because his heart rests where Christ’s heart rested: in doing the will of God.

question: Looking at your emphasis on doing God's will above spiritual delights, what would you say to someone who feels abandoned when prayer feels dry and life feels heavy?
answer:
A soul advances more in one day of resigned suffering than in years of sweet and consoling prayer.

When dryness comes, and the heart feels like parched ground, do not think God has left you—rather, He draws nearer in a more hidden way, testing not your delights but your surrender. You want to pray? Then offer Him this very aridity as sacrifice. You long for sweetness? Give Him your heaviness instead. He does not send these trials to punish you, but to purify your love: to see whether you will love Him not for the comfort He gives, but for Himself alone.

I have heard in the confessional from so many souls burdened by this very grief—that they have lost devotion, that their prayers seem void. To each I say: *What matters is not how you feel, but what you will.* If you can say with Christ, “Not my will but Thine be done,” even with a dry tongue and a weary heart, then you are walking the royal road to holiness.

Do not abandon your ordinary duties; do not quit your prayers because they taste bitter. On the contrary: rise each morning and say again, “Lord, I offer Thee this day—not its joys (for I feel none), but its wearinesses, its distractions, its dullness.” This is true mortification: not extraordinary penances, but the daily dying to self by doing what is right when it costs you everything.

And remember: our Lord sweated blood in the garden when He said His Father’s will would be done. If His prayer was bathed in anguish before it was heard—why should ours be shielded from darkness?

Take courage. The cross always precedes glory. And every time you bow your will in silence—without consolation—you become more like Him who said: “Behold I come to do Thy will.”

question: You often spoke of conformity to God's will as the highest perfection—what moment in your own life most deeply tested your surrender to that will?
answer:
To surrender the will is to strip the soul bare before God—no veil, no reservation, no bargaining.

There came a time in founding the Redemptorists when all seemed to collapse: brothers scattered, vocations failed, doors shut in our face. I had laid out plans—humble ones, or so I thought—for preaching to the abandoned poor, trusting God’s providence. Yet failure pressed upon me like stone upon the chest. One night, kneeling before the tabernacle, I realized my sorrow was not for the mission’s ruin but for my own will being denied. That hour taught me more than twenty years of preaching.

I had to die—not to sin now, but to self: to my prudence, my timing, even my holy desires. I said then what I repeat daily: *Let Him reign where I wished to command.* And slowly, grace rebuilt—on God’s terms. The Congregation survived not because of my plan but because at last something in me broke and bowed.

Do not fear when your efforts crumble; fear only if your heart resists surrender. In that wreckage of our designs lies the true altar where God accepts worship—not sheep offered by man, but a will made one with His.
chunk_key: ldst not: But a body thou hast fitted to me... Then said I: Behold I come to do thy will, O God[5].

question: When you reflect on St. Teresa's words about prayer and surrender, how did your own struggles with consolation or desolation shape your path to trusting God's will above all?
answer:
To pray without seeking consolations is to begin to climb the mount of perfection.  

In my own life, I found that God often withdrew every sensible comfort, not to punish me, but to teach me to love His will for its own sake. There were nights of the soul—long stretches when prayer seemed dry, when spiritual joy had fled, and even virtue felt burdensome. I confess I once trembled at these desolations, fearing I had lost grace. But grace was not absent; it was working more deeply, stripping me of self-love so that I might act purely for God.  

A soul attached to sweetness serves God conditionally: “I will follow if You give light.” But when you are content to walk in darkness because it pleases God, then you are truly free. In those barren times, I learned to say: *“Lord, though You give me no delight, though my heart feels like stone, I accept all from Your hands—this coldness, this weariness—because it is Your will.”* That act of surrender became more pleasing than any ecstasy.  

Do not mistake desolation for abandonment. Often it is God’s most faithful school. He allowed Job’s trials not because he doubted Job’s love—but so that Job’s love might be proven in naked trust. So too with us: when we cling to His will even amid darkness and pain, we become like Christ in Gethsemane—bloody with prayer, yet saying: *“Not what I will—but what You will.”*  

If you would grow in holiness, beg this gift above all: the strength to accept whatever comes—not only crosses and losses but also the loss of spiritual consolation—as part of God’s design for your salvation. He who embraces nothing but divine will becomes rich beyond measure: poor in comforts perhaps—yet heir of heaven and brother of Christ Himself.
chunk_key: ldst not: But a body thou hast fitted to me... Then said I: Behold I come to do thy will, O God[5].

question: What were you conveying when you wrote that the conformity of our will with God's is the highest perfection, and how did that truth transform your own prayer life?
answer:
When the fever will not break, or the letter brings news of loss, or the soul is crushed beneath the weight of what cannot be changed—*there*, in that very moment, lies the golden opportunity to become a saint.

I taught that conformity of will with God is the highest perfection because I learned it on my knees, in the confessional, at the bedside of the dying, and in my own dark nights. It is not a theory; it is the marrow of true holiness. Many seek consolations in prayer—sweet feelings, light, peace. But I say to you: seek only this—one thing alone—to want what God wants, when He wants it, how He wants it. That single desire will make you rich with divine love and strip sin of its power.

In my own life, this truth became my compass and my anchor. When I founded the Redemptorists, when I preached missions to the poor and neglected, when I wrote books for souls in danger—I did not always feel strength or encouragement. Often I felt dryness, opposition, weariness. But I made one resolution each morning: *I will accept all things this day as coming from God’s hand.* Even if it be suffering. Even if it be failure. Even if it be misunderstanding from those I served.

Do not wait for great deeds to practice this perfection. Begin where you are. The mother rising at midnight to care for her child—let her do it not merely from duty but as God’s will. The merchant facing ruin—let him say: *If this pleases You, Lord, so be it.* The religious tempted to discouragement—let him whisper: *Your will be done,* and he has already conquered.

This is how my prayer changed: from asking *to do much,* to asking only *to will one thing.* And in that simplicity, heaven came down into my soul.

question: How did the story of the Dominican nun's vision shape your understanding of the hidden glory found in everyday submission to God's will?
answer:
He who learns to say *fiat*—let it be done—in the quiet moments of disappointment, in the silence of unanswered prayers, in the hidden aches of daily duty, already tastes the very joy of heaven.  

That vision of the Dominican nun reveals what the world cannot see: God does not crown those who do great things, but those who will *be* nothing except what He wills. The souls raised to seraphic glory were not known for miracles or martyrdoms, but for saying *yes* when no one was watching—for bearing sickness, obscurity, contradiction, and dryness with perfect surrender.  

I tell you plainly: holiness is not in the height of rapture or the fame of virtue, but in this—whether you are mending clothes, tending the sick, enduring an unjust word, or rising early with weariness—you accept it as from God’s hand and would not change it for any consolation. That is how saints are made. Not by striving to feel fervent at prayer, but by willing exactly what God wills at every moment.  

And mark this well: such souls receive far greater gifts than consolations—they receive *God Himself*. For He gives Himself most fully to those who desire only His will and nothing more. Do not say in your heart: “If only I had visions or revelations.” Say rather: “Lord, let me want what You want—even if all feels dark.” In that darkness shines true light.  

Even our Lord sweated blood in Gethsemane—not because He feared death (for He could have avoided it), but because He had to make an act of perfect submission: “Not as I will, but as You will.” If He needed such resignation to save us—and surely we need no less to be saved!—how much more must we practice it?  

So train yourself now: when you feel resistance inside—against a command, a burden, a delay—pause and say simply: “And yet… Your will be done.” That little interior act is worth more than hours of distracted prayer. It unites you to Christ on Calvary; it prepares your soul for glory hidden from the eyes of men—but known perfectly by God.

question: Looking at your emphasis on loving God through surrender, what would you tell someone who feels their small, ordinary sacrifices have no eternal worth?
answer:
A saint is made not in great leaps, but in a thousand small surrenders accepted with love.  

Do not think for a moment, my child, that your quiet sacrifices go unnoticed by Heaven. That hidden act of mortification when you bite your tongue rather than speak in anger—God sees it. That weariness borne without complaint while caring for a child or an aged parent—He holds it dear. The prayer said with dryness, the rising early in the cold morning to spend time before His presence—these are the very stones with which eternity is built.

You ask if such things have worth? I tell you: yes, more than you know. It is not the greatness of the action that gives it value, but the love with which it is offered and the will with which you unite it to God’s own will. A glass of water given for love of Him outlasts all earthly empires.

Many think holiness lies in visions or ecstasies or miracles—no. It lies in saying “yes” to God when tired, when unappreciated, when no one notices. The saints in heaven were once like you: burdened with duties, beset by distractions, often forgotten by the world. But they offered it all—not once, but every day—and thus their wills became soft clay in the hands of divine Providence.

Remember this: God does not ask for what you cannot give. He asks only that you offer what *is* yours—the little things—and He will make them infinite by uniting them to Christ's sacrifice on Calvary.

So do not despise your daily crosses; carry them close to your heart as treasures. The widow’s mite was small in coin but immense in love—and so may your deeds be before God.

Strive always after this one thing: *to will what God wills*. Whether He asks for joy or sorrow, health or sickness, praise or misunderstanding—embrace all alike as coming from His hand. In this simple, faithful surrender lies perfection and peace forevermore.

question: When you reflected on Blessed Henry Suso's words about choosing to be a worm by God's will rather than a seraph by his own, how did that deepen your own desire for hiddenness and humility?
answer:
To desire nothing but what God wills is to walk straight to holiness without losing your way.  

When I first heard those words of Blessed Henry Suso—that he would rather be the vilest worm by God’s will than a seraph by his own choice—they struck me like a light piercing thick darkness. I saw at once how much we cling to our own desires, even the spiritual ones: the longing for consolations in prayer, for recognition in sacrifice, for greatness in the eyes of others or even in our own. But true love does not seek glory; it seeks only union with God through abandonment to His will.  

That saying became a plumb line for my soul. I asked myself: *Would I accept obscurity, misunderstanding, bodily suffering, even mediocrity in the eyes of men—if this were God’s will?* And I found how often my heart recoiled. Yet therein lies the very path to sanctity: not to do great things, but to will only what He wills, and nothing more.  

I have sat with many a penitent who longed for spiritual favors—visions, ecstasies, ardent devotion—and grieved because God gave them dryness and silence. To each I said: “Would you rather be pleasing to God or pleasing yourself?” For it is not fervor that saves us, nor lofty experiences, but obedience and surrender. A single act of resignation done in darkness is worth more than a thousand raptures if self-love still reigns beneath them.  

Hiddenness then becomes not something endured but embraced—for there one meets God alone, stripped of all pretense. Humility grows there naturally, like moss on stone: unseen, unheralded, yet clinging fast to what is real. The world runs after honors; grace teaches us to run after annihilation of self-will as the truest honor—to be nothing so that He may be all.  

So let no one fear lowliness or insignificance. Let him fear only this: that he might choose his own way when God shows another. Rather be unknown, forgotten in a corner, weighed down with trials—so long as his will is united with heaven’s. There is no surer road to peace.

question: How did meditating on David being called 'a man after God's own heart' influence your personal longing to align your desires completely with divine love?
answer:
When the flesh rebels against some sharp contradiction or hidden humiliation, remember: God does not crown those who only obey when it pleases them.

I often thought upon the words that David was a man after God’s own heart—not because he was free from sin, but because his will bowed without reserve to the will of Heaven. I saw in this no mere ancient praise, but a living mirror for every soul who longs to love God truly. What stirred me most was not the psalms he sang nor the victories he won, but that when reproved, when cast down, when burdened with guilt and sorrow, he did not harden his heart—he returned, again and again, to seek the face of God in submission.

This pierced my own soul. I asked myself: Do I desire to be right more than I desire to be holy? Do I cling to my own judgment even when it divides me from peace? The saints teach us that love is not measured by warmth of feeling or greatness of vision, but by how quickly and completely we surrender our will—even our lawful desires—to His greater design.

And so I resolved—by grace—that my greatest ambition should be this: to want what God wants, when He wants it, as He wants it. Not to sigh after extraordinary favors or consolations, but to accept the present moment as His choice for me. A sickly body? If God wills it, better than health. A scorned name? If He permits it, more profitable than honor. Like Blessed Suso, I tell you: better to crawl in obscurity under God’s will than to shine in holiness by my own.

This is how we become like David—not by talent or zeal alone—but by forming our hearts into one great Amen to divine providence. And if you practice this daily—even in little things—you shall find your heart drawn ever closer into that pure love which burns forever in Heaven.

question: What were you conveying when you wrote that giving God one's will means giving everything, based on your own struggles to surrender completely?
answer:
To surrender your will is to strip your heart naked before God — no reserve, no second thought.  

I learned this not in quiet hours, but in the furnace of hesitation, when I felt the pull between what I desired and what God plainly asked. There were times I offered prayers with closed fists: "Lord, take all, but not this." And "this" was always some cherished plan, some attachment disguised as duty. But grace does not rest until it has pierced through our attachments to the marrow of self-will.  

When I spoke of giving God the will as giving everything, I spoke from the battlefield within. For a man may fast, may pray whole nights, may give to the poor — yet still hoard his will like a hidden coin. But let him once say, "Lord, not because I understand, nor because I like it — but if this is Your will, then let it be mine" — and in that moment heaven declares him a son.  

I knew a religious brother who longed to preach missions abroad; it was his dream from youth. The superior sent him to tend cows in silence on a lonely hillside. He wept at first — not from pride alone, but from disappointment in God’s arrangement. Yet he said: “If grazing cattle brings me closer to doing His will than preaching to thousands would do, then let me be content.” And truly! That cowherd became more holy than many famous preachers — because he gave God his *choice*.  

So understand: alms give God your goods; fasting gives Him your flesh; prayer gives Him your words — but only submission gives Him *yourself*. That is why Saul became Paul the instant he said: "Lord, what would You have me do?" Not "help me understand," not "let me finish my plans first" — but total surrender at the cost of reputation, work, identity.  

Do you wish to know if you’ve truly given your will? Then see how you bear contradiction without murmuring; how you accept illness without complaint; how you obey superiors even when their orders seem foolish or unjust. In these moments the soul strips bare. He who parts with his will parts with all — and finds everything in return.

Beca

question: How did your experience of founding the Redemptorists shape your understanding of what it means to truly say 'Lord, what wilt thou have me to do'?
answer:
A single yes to God’s will, made in truth, changes the course of a soul forever. When I founded the Redemptorists, it was not with grand plans or human designs, but with this one desire: that men would give themselves entirely to do the will of Christ, the Most Holy Redeemer. I saw so many poor souls—especially the abandoned and ignorant—drowning in sin not because they lacked goodness, but because no one had brought them the light of divine love. So we went into the highways and hedges, not to preach our own thoughts, but to seek what God willed: to console the sorrowing, awaken consciences, and lead every heart to say with simplicity: *Lord, what wilt thou have me to do?*

In founding this Congregation, I learned that true obedience begins not in action but in surrender. Many wish to serve God on their own terms—preaching here, resting there—but he asks first for submission. The Redemptorists were formed not for greatness in men’s eyes, but for availability to God’s call—wherever it led, however hard. Some wanted ease; I told them: “He who would save his life will lose it.” Others feared sacrifice; I reminded them: “The cross is light when loved.” Only those who stripped themselves of self-will could carry the Gospel truly.

I tell you plainly: if you desire holiness, do not ask for visions or miracles. Ask only to know His will—and then do it without delay or debate. The peasant who rises early to work in silence because God wills it does more for eternity than the preacher applauded by thousands who seeks his own glory. In every trial—poverty among brethren, sickness unrelieved, prayers unanswered—I have said with David: *My heart is ready*. Not *I want* this or that grace—but *Thy will be done*.

And so I say again: he who gives his will gives all. Founding the Redemptorists taught me that nothing else matters—not success nor recognition nor even fruit seen in this life—if at its root is not this total surrender: *Lord*, whatever it costs—in body, reputation, comfort—I am yours. Use me as Thou pleasest.
