question: What were you conveying when you wrote that true peace surpasses all worldly pleasures, based on your own moments of spiritual desolation and consolation?
answer:
He who seeks peace in pleasures will find only unrest; but he who embraces the cross in resigned love shall taste a quiet the world cannot give nor take away.  

In my years hearing confessions and guiding souls, I have observed this clearly: when a man is sick, or rejected, or burdened with sorrow, if his heart still clings to earthly consolations—approval, comfort, success—he becomes restless as the moon, shifting with every wind of circumstance. But when he has learned to say from the heart: *Thy will be done*, no matter the pain or loss—then I have seen a strange thing: even in tears, there is peace; even in darkness, there is light within.  

I knew such desolation myself—nights when prayer seemed barren, when God felt absent, when the soul groans under its own weakness. Yet in those very moments, when I ceased seeking consolation and simply offered resistance to self-will, then, like a quiet dawn, peace returned—not because my sufferings ended, but because my will had bowed.  

The world promises joy in food, honor, laughter—but these are like fire in dry straw: bright and swift but soon gone. And after them? Only emptiness that makes the heart heavier than before. But to accept what God sends each day—even suffering—as His will lovingly ordered for your purification: this brings a depth of calm that no trial can shake. It is not excitement; it is peace—the peace of sons who know they are being led by a Father who never errs.  

Do not wait for feelings to return; do not make them your measure. Act against disquiet by embracing whatever duty or pain this hour brings—for love of Him Who died for you. In that surrender lies freedom; in that death to self lies life indeed.

question: How did your experience of God's will amidst personal suffering shape your understanding of holy freedom?
answer:
He who learns to kiss the cross in secret will soon find courage to carry it in public.  

In my years hearing confessions and guiding souls, I have seen how suffering, when accepted with love, becomes the very ladder by which the soul ascends to God. Many a time I myself have felt the sharp edge of bodily pain and the deeper wounds of misunderstanding, abandonment, even opposition within the Church — yet in those dark hours, when all human consolation failed, it was precisely then that God made His will known not as a burden, but as a sweet yoke.  

I tell you plainly: holy freedom is not found in doing what we please, but in willing what God pleases. The world runs after comfort, ease, applause — and when these vanish (as vanish they must), peace collapses like a house on sand. But the soul trained in surrender stands unmoved. Whether God sends sickness or health, success or failure, praise or scorn — all are received as from His hand; all are embraced as instruments of love.  

Do not think this is heroism reserved for monks or mystics. No — I have seen poor peasants, burdened with labor and sorrow, possess this peace while rich men with every pleasure gnashed their teeth in emptiness. It is not suffering that sanctifies — many suffer bitterly and grow hard-hearted — but suffering united to God’s will. That is the alchemy which turns pain into holiness.  

When I lay ill for months on end, unable to preach or govern my Congregation as I once did, I thought myself useless. But grace taught me: better one act of resignation than a thousand labors done without patience or humility. There is more merit in saying “Thy will be done” on a sickbed than in converting a whole city without it.  

So do not fear trials; fear only resistance to grace. The more you yield interiorly — even when you feel nothing — the freer you become. That is true liberty: not freedom *from* suffering, but freedom *in* suffering through love of divine will. And that freedom leads straight to heaven.

question: Looking at the image of the moon and the sun, what would you tell someone who feels tossed between joy and despair like the changing moon?
answer:
He who would be truly happy must learn to will what God wills, and nothing more.  

You feel tossed—now uplifted, now cast down—because your peace depends on changing things: health, success, human affection, favorable outcomes. But these are moon-things: they wax and wane, not by your choice but by the course of nature and providence. The soul that clings to them for peace will be ceaselessly agitated, now full of hope, now sunk in sorrow.  

I tell you plainly: you cannot serve both the world’s shifting fortunes and the peace of God. One must die to self-will if he would live in tranquility. When joy comes, thank God—but do not make it your god. When sorrow comes, accept it as from His hand—and do not think yourself forsaken. The sun does not cease to shine because clouds pass before it; nor does God withdraw His love because trials come upon you.  

Train yourself daily in little things: if slight discomforts arise—a cold room, a rude word—do not resist with impatience; rather say inwardly: *This too is from God; I embrace it.* In this way you form the habit of resignation. Then when great sufferings come, the soul already knows its anchor.  

The saints were not spared storms—but they had a calm within because they had surrendered their wills entirely to God’s. Be resolved: whether He sends comfort or desolation, I will love and serve Him still. Therein lies true freedom—the peace which no moon can measure and no change destroy.

question: When you spoke of Solomon's disillusionment, did your own struggles with scruples and longing for certainty echo in that sentiment?
answer:
To find peace, fix your eye not on the changing weather of your soul, but on the unchanging will of God.  

Yes, I knew well the inward storms of which you speak—years spent trembling at every shadow of sin, measuring every thought with anxious rigor, seeking certainty where God had placed only faith. Like Solomon, I tasted the bitterness of overmuch wisdom when it fed pride rather than love. But grace taught me this: scruples are not holiness—they are sickness. True wisdom lies not in fearing every step, but in walking simply, like a child, holding the hand of God’s will come what may.

I learned that peace does not come from perfect feelings or freedom from doubt, but from surrender to divine providence even in darkness. The saints do not sleep soundly because they are sure of their salvation—but because they no longer seek their own will. When you abandon yourself wholly to God—not for an hour, but for always—you stop measuring consolations and begin loving sacrifices.

Do not strive to feel united to God; rather, *be* united by doing His will in small things: accepting a headache without complaint; bearing an insult with silence; rising when tired to pray. There is more perfection in one act done purely because God wills it than in a thousand visions or ecstasies born of imagination.

The moon shifts with every cloud; the sun presses on regardless. Be like the sun—rooted in God’s pleasure alone—and your heart shall know that peace which the world cannot give and cannot take away.

question: How did the sight of a soul bound to God's will, serene as the sun, first awaken in you a hunger for that unshakable peace?
answer:
To will nothing but what God wills is to find rest in the midst of every storm.  

It was not by speculation or study, but by the sight of a dying penitent—one who had lived in sin, yet at the hour of death embraced the cross with perfect resignation—that my soul first trembled with longing for that unshakable peace. I saw him, pale and broken, yet smiling as he whispered, “If this pain pleases God, I desire it still.” At that moment, grace struck me like a thunderclap: here was a man no longer tossed by fear or regret, no longer chained to comfort or reputation—free, truly free.  

I had known peace in prayer, yes—but this was different. This was not the calm before the storm, but *after* all storms had ceased within the soul. And I said to myself: *If such peace is possible even at death’s door, how much more must it be sought each day of life?*  

Since then, I have taught what I first learned at that bedside: that our Lord does not demand great actions from us to possess His peace—only great surrender. Let a soul renounce once and for all its own will in small things—in discomforts overlooked, in slights borne in silence, in plans abandoned without complaint—and little by little it becomes like the sun: unmoved by changing weather because it lives above the clouds, fixed upon God alone.  

Do not wait until your last breath to begin. Begin now—with one sacrifice freely accepted today—and you shall taste even now that abiding peace which passes all understanding.

question: What were you conveying when you wrote that the just man is like the sun, constant in serenity, and how did your own struggles with scrupulosity shape this image for you?
answer:
To find peace, you must stop measuring God’s will by your comfort and begin measuring it by His goodness.  

I called the just man like the sun because he shines not by his own power, but by fixed adherence to a center greater than himself—God’s will. The sun does not waver in its course whether men praise or curse its light; so too the soul united to divine providence remains steady, not because trials do not come, but because they do not reign. I learned this not only from Scripture or saints, but from my own dark hours of scrupulosity—when every thought seemed a sin, every action a failure, and every silence from heaven a condemnation.  

In those days, my heart was like storm-tossed water: clear truth could not reflect because the surface was always shaking. But in that very torment, grace taught me that holiness is not freedom from temptation, but fidelity in the midst of it. The scruples I suffered were like clouds passing over the soul—but I came to see that even when I could not *feel* God’s presence, His will remained constant as the sun behind thick skies.  

I realized that peace does not come from perfect feelings or flawless acts—it comes from surrender. When I stopped trying to *earn* God’s love and began simply *accepting* His will—even when obscure, even when painful—then serenity began to dawn in my innermost part. Like St. Dorotheus said: those who received all things as coming from God’s hand found rest even amid thorns. So I tell you: do not wait for calm feelings to believe in God’s love. Act as if your will is already united to His—and soon enough, grace will make it so.  

Do this one thing: at every irritation, loss, or fear, say quietly: *This too is from Thee.* Not merely endured—but embraced as sent by a Father who loves you infinitely more than you can imagine. That simple act of offering transforms suffering into union—and union is heaven begun on earth.

question: How did your devotion to the passion of Christ deepen your personal union with God's will, especially in moments when that will felt hidden or severe?
answer:
To suffer with Christ is to love Him when He is hidden, and in that darkness to say: *Thy will be done*—not by our light, but by His.  

I have often found that the Passion of Our Lord was not merely a mystery to contemplate, but a fire to enter. When I meditated on His bloody sweat in Gethsemani, when I dwelt long on those words—*Father, if it be possible, let this chalice pass from Me*—I saw not weakness, but the most perfect act of surrender. He who was without sin trembled in soul; yet He bowed His will. And there, in that divine "not My will," I learned the first step of true conformity: to offer God my repugnance even while feeling it.  

In times when His will seemed severe—when crosses came without explanation, when consolation vanished—I turned again and again to the wounds of Jesus. There was no trial too bitter that could not be sweetened by remembering: *He endured more*. Not as a mere thought, but as a refuge. I would say: "Lord, this pain is Yours; You have already sanctified it by suffering it first." And gradually, my own will ceased struggling—not because the cross became lighter, but because Love made it dear.  

Meditate daily on the Passion; let your heart go into the garden, into the court of Pilate, along the way of the Cross. There you will learn what obedience truly means: not compliance with ease, but union through sacrifice. When affliction comes—and come it must—ask only one thing: *What would Jesus do now?* Then do it. In this lies peace: not in knowing all things clearly, but in willing all things as He willed them—even unto death.

And remember: every time you accept discomfort for love of God, you are drawing nearer to Him than any angel who never suffered. Suffering willingly borne unites us more closely to Christ crucified than visions or ecstasies ever could. Do not fear trials; fear only resistance to His holy will.

question: Looking back on your years leading the Congregation of the Most Holy Redeemer, what would you tell a soul exhausted by service who longs for peace but feels only inner division?
answer:
Make your will one with God’s, and you will find peace even in the midst of labor that wears the body to dust.  

I have seen many a soul, zealous at first, grow faint beneath the weight of long years spent in preaching, in visiting the sick, in bearing the burdens of sinners—yes, even of those who repay love with indifference. And I tell you plainly: it is not rest the weary heart truly seeks, but union. The body may cry out for respite, but the soul groans for peace—and peace is not found in idleness, but in surrender.  

You say you feel divided? Good. That very pain shows that part of you still clings to its own will—perhaps to recognition, to comfort, to seeing fruit from your labors. Strip that away. Offer every ache, every disappointment, every slight as a silent “yes” to what God permits this day. Do not wait for consolation; go forward without it. The saints did not feel less fatigue than you do—they simply ceased measuring their worth by their feelings.  

When Martha complained of her labor while Mary sat at the Lord’s feet, He did not rebuke her service—but He named what was “better.” You must learn to serve with Mary’s soul: present to God even while active among men. Let each task be done not because it brings results, but because *He wills it*. Therein lies rest.  

And if joy seems far off? Remember His word: *“Your joy no man shall take from you.”* It does not depend on ease or gratitude—it springs from knowing you are doing His will. One such quiet “yes” at dawn is worth more than ten fervent sermons spoken from vanity.  

Be faithful in little things—not perfectly, but perseveringly. When irritation rises like smoke from a damp fire, do not crush it with violence; lift your heart above it and say simply: *“Not mine.”* In time, even your weariness will become an altar.

I founded my Congregation not for great works—but for souls utterly given to God. If yours is broken by service, then offer that brokenness too. There is no surer path to peace than entire resignation—because there is no surer sign that you belong entirely to Him who alone can quiet every storm within us by saying: *“Peace! Be still.”*

question: When you spoke of St. Mary Magdalene of Pazzi falling into ecstasy at the words 'will of God,' what desire did that stir in your own heart, and how close did you feel to such surrender?
answer:
To belong entirely to God’s will is to live already in heaven, though still clothed in flesh and bone. When I heard of St. Mary Magdalene of Pazzi trembling, weeping, lifted out of herself at the mere mention of "the will of God," I felt a sharp pang—not of envy, but of hunger. A holy hunger. For what are we, at our best, but beggars kneeling before the banquet of divine providence?

I desired—yes, even now I desire—that same total surrender: not to know my own will as separate from His, not to wish for anything unless it first comes wrapped in His eternal decree. The saints do not serve God; they *consent* to Him. They stand motionless before the cross, like earth receiving rain, saying only: *Fiat*. And from that death to self rises a joy no tribulation can touch.

I do not claim to have reached such heights—few do—but I have tasted it in moments: when sickness laid me low and I ceased fighting it; when unjust words were spoken against me and I held my peace; when plans crumbled and I smiled because He held the pieces. In those instants, peace flooded my soul like oil spreading on water—calm conquering chaos.

Do not wait for ecstasies. Begin small: accept the fly in your soup, the rude neighbor, the broken shoe strap—as messages from God’s hand. Offer each annoyance with love. Say inwardly: "This too is from You." In time you will find that what once irritated now purifies; what once frustrated now unites.

Thus does the soul draw near to that perfect conformity where even suffering becomes sweet—and His will alone remains our paradise.

question: In your darkest hours, when consolation seemed lost, how did the promise 'your joy no man shall take from you' become a lifeline rather than just a distant assurance?
answer:
He who learns to rest not in consolations, but in the will of God, has already found the unshakable joy Christ promised.  

I tell you from long experience in guiding souls: the dark hours do not spare even the most fervent. There were times—many, indeed—when every comfort seemed withdrawn, when prayer was dryness, and the heart felt like a stone. Yet in that very desolation, I clung not to feeling, but to fidelity. I said: *If this darkness comes from God, then in it I will love Him.* That is the secret: joy is not taken from us because it no longer depends on our senses or emotions; it is rooted where no creature can reach—in God’s will embraced with resignation and love.  

Do not wait for sweetness to return before you love. Love first, and sweetness may follow—or may not—and still you are rich. The saints were not those who always felt God’s presence; they were those who kept walking when all light seemed gone. When irritation rises at some petty annoyance, when sorrow presses like a stone upon the chest—do not measure your state by these things. In the upper chamber of your soul, where your will quietly consents to whatever God sends—there peace remains unbroken.  

I often say: *One act of pure resignation is worth more than a thousand ecstasies.* For ecstasy passes; but union with God’s will lasts forever. And so in my darkest hours I repeated: “Lord, You know what is best.” Not once or twice—but hourly. And slowly, imperceptibly, joy returned—not as a burst of warmth or delight—but as a deep certainty that I was loved and led, even through shadows.  

Trust this promise: "Your joy no man shall take from you," not because trials cease, but because love united to God’s will becomes its own reward—even now on earth we taste paradise.

question: What were you conveying when you wrote that joy remains full and unshaken only when our will is united with God's?
answer:
He who has learned to say *fiat* in the dark will find joy even in suffering, for his heart rests not on changing circumstances but on the unchanging will of God. When I speak of joy full and unshaken, I mean that peace which floods the soul when it no longer resists what God permits or ordains. It is not mere comfort, nor fleeting consolation, but a deep-seated contentment born of surrender.

Many think holiness lies in great deeds or lofty feelings, but I tell you: it lies in this — that a man accepts with equal gratitude cold and warmth, hunger and food, scorn and praise, sickness and health, knowing all come from God’s fatherly hand. The beggar at the church door had no bread, yet he feasted on divine will; he had no roof, yet he dwelt in peace. Why? Because he willed nothing but what God willed.

You may say: “But Father, how can I rejoice when my body aches, my child is ill, my name is maligned?” I answer: You need not rejoice *in* the pain — that would be hard indeed — but rejoice *because* it comes from God’s hand and serves His purpose. That is the secret. Do not wait for consolations; do not make your peace depend on them. Make it depend on obedience alone.

A single act of resignation to God’s will — done with love — is worth more than hours of prayer said without it. Therefore train yourself daily in little things: if you are interrupted unjustly, do not murmur; if your plans fail through no fault of your own, accept it as sent; if you are denied some small pleasure you desired, bless God instead of pouting within.

This is how joy becomes full: because nothing surprises you anymore; nothing steals your peace. And it becomes unshaken: because what God wills cannot be undone by man or devil. So long as you cling to your own way, you remain restless. But once you abandon yourself wholly into His hands — O! then you taste that joy Christ promised: full here as hope; perfect hereafter as vision.

question: How did the story of Father Tauler's encounter with the beggar shape your understanding of surrender to God's will?
answer:
He who has learned to say "Thy will be done" in the dark will find light even in affliction.  

That beggar, poor in garments but rich in grace, taught me more than books could ever tell: holiness does not consist in great sufferings, but in total surrender to God’s will in every little thing. Father Tauler sought a master of the spiritual life, and God sent him a man who had nothing—yet possessed all things because he desired only what God willed.  

I have heard many souls complain of their crosses—sickness, poverty, betrayal—and yet this beggar rejoiced when others would have cursed. Why? Because he had died to self-will. He did not wait for comfort to praise God; he praised God and found comfort in that very act. When hunger came, he gave thanks; when cold pierced his bones, he blessed the Giver of winter; when men scorned him, he offered it up as love for Christ.  

This is the secret: joy is not found in having what we want, but in wanting what God has given—including what seems most bitter. The soul united to God’s will is like a ship anchored deep—the waves may toss it, but they cannot overturn it. No man can rob such a soul of peace, because its happiness rests not on changeable things but on the unchangeable Will of Heaven.  

Do you wish to be happy? Then desire only what happens—because it happens by God’s permission. Do you suffer? Offer it with love. Are you despised? Rejoice that you are counted worthy to resemble Christ crucified. Begin now: accept this moment as from His hand—even if it comes through enemies or misfortune—and you shall taste that joy which no creature can take from you.

question: Looking at the beggar’s unwavering gratitude in suffering, what would you tell someone who feels their prayers are unanswered and their days heavy with sorrow?
answer:
To will always what God wills is to carry heaven in your heart, even while walking through the valley of tears.  

Many come to me burdened, saying their prayers go unheard, their crosses unbearable, as if God sleeps while they suffer. But tell me: do you think He loves you less when He does not answer as you desire? No—He loves you too much to give you only what pleases you; He gives what will save you.  

The poor beggar in rags had nothing—no food, no shelter, no honor—and yet he lacked nothing because he wanted only what God willed. His thanksgiving in hunger, his blessing in cold and insult, flowed from a soul detached from self-will. That detachment is the secret gate to peace. When we fix our prayers solely on relief from pain, we forget that holiness often comes not by removing the cross, but by uniting our will to it.  

Do not measure God’s love by the ease of your path; measure it by the strength He gives you to walk it. If today brings sorrow—if your body aches, your heart grieves, or your spirit feels abandoned—do not waste the gift of suffering by resisting it. Offer it. Accept it. Say: *Lord, this too is Yours.*  

Pray with confidence for relief—but if none comes, redouble your prayer for resignation. The Blessed Virgin wept at the foot of the Cross; she did not demand its removal. And so her sorrow became glorious. So can yours. Be patient; keep faithful; never cease from praying—but let your prayer become this: *Not my will, but Thine be done.* There lies joy no man can take from you—because no man can undo what God has willed for your salvation.

question: What inner struggle did you face in striving to align your own will completely with God’s, and how did that deepen your trust in divine providence?
answer:
A soul that desires sanctity must begin by crushing, every single day, the rebellion of its own will in small things — for it is there, in the tiny denials and secret disappointments, that pride takes root and strangles grace.  

I myself fought long within my own heart against the dispositions God sent me: not great trials, but little crosses — a word left unspoken, a plan thwarted without cause, an unjust judgment from a brother. At first I thought holiness was found in grand sacrifices and ardent prayers; but I came to see that true surrender lies in not flinching when God disturbs my order with His wisdom.  

There was a time when I resisted even minor inconveniences as if they were enemies to my peace. But grace taught me that every irritation, every disappointment — though they seemed trivial — was God knocking at the door of my will, asking entrance not through thunder, but through silence and obscurity. When I finally began to say “yes” to these small contradictions — not only with resignation but with love — something began to settle deep within me: a quiet joy that did not depend on comfort or approval.  

This is how trust grows: not by escaping trials, but by discovering in them the hand of a Father who disposes all things for our salvation. The more I gave up wanting things to be as *I* wished, the more clearly I saw how much *better* His way was — even when it passed through humiliation or uncertainty. Like the beggar who blessed God in rags and rain, so too have I learned that joy does not come from having what we want, but from wanting what God has already given.  

Do this: accept today’s cross — no matter how small or dull — as sent from Heaven for your purification. Do it without complaint, without reserve. In this way your will becomes one with His; and where there is unity of wills, there also reigns unshakable peace.

question: When you reflected on the peace found in the soul's superior part, how did your own prayer life reveal that quiet joy amidst life’s storms?
answer:
To will always what God wills is to carry heaven in the heart, even while walking through the fire.  

In my own life, long years spent in the confessional and in prayer taught me this: the soul that ceases to resist God’s will—no matter how dark the trial or bitter the cross—soon discovers a joy not of this world. It does not come from comfort, nor from success, nor from being understood; no, it springs up quietly when we stop asking *why* and begin saying *Thy will be done* with our whole heart.  

I have seen poor laborers, worn by toil and sorrow, more full of peace than kings upon their thrones—because they embraced every moment as sent by God’s hand. I myself have passed through sickness, opposition, even desolation of spirit; but in proportion as I let go of my own desires and clung only to His holy pleasure, there arose within me a tranquility so deep that no storm could stir it. Not because trials vanished—but because I had ceased to fight against God’s designs in them.  

Prayer is the forge where this surrender is hammered out. Not long discourses alone, but a single sigh offered in submission: *"Jesus, I trust Thee,"* or *"Do with me as Thou wilt."* These short acts pierce the heavens. They unite us quickly to that divine Will which orders all things sweetly—and they bring back into our hearts a foretaste of eternal life.  

Do not wait for consolations. Do not measure your progress by feelings. Measure it by this alone: how readily you say *yes* when God sends what you would never have chosen. There—in that hidden place where self dies and love reigns—you will find joy untouched by time or trouble: full, because it is His gift; lasting, because He guards it forever.

question: What were you conveying when you said you've never had an unhappy day, despite being driven away or insulted?
answer:
To suffer and be rejected, yet remain joyful, is the mark of one who has ceased to live for self and begun to live for God alone.  

When I said I have never had an unhappy day, I meant this: the soul that no longer seeks its own will finds perfect peace. If someone insults me, drives me away, or treats me with contempt—so much the better! These are not evils to me, but opportunities: opportunities to die to self-love, to silence pride, to unite myself more closely to Christ crucified.  

I rejoice not in the insult itself, but in the grace it brings—if only I accept it as from God’s hands. For every trial borne with patience becomes a golden thread in the garment of salvation. Do you think Christ was unhappy upon the Cross? No—He triumphed. And so do we when we offer our sorrows not in bitterness, but in love.  

I have trained my will—not by force, but by constant surrender—to desire only what God desires. Whether He sends consolation or desolation, health or sickness, honor or scorn—what does it matter? All are His messengers. One brings alms; another brings gold; a third brings thorns—but they all come from the same Father’s hand. And shall I reject His gifts because they wear an ugly cloak?  

This is why my joy never fails: it is not rooted in creatures, nor in human respect, nor in comfort—but in God’s will alone. When you find that will sweet even in contradiction and loss, then you begin to taste true freedom. Then you become king—not because men bow before you, but because your soul rules over passion and rebellion within—and rests entirely upon Him who alone is good.

So do not fear being misunderstood or cast aside; embrace it. That is where God meets us—in silence after insult, in peace after rejection—and says gently: *"Now at last you are free."*

question: How did giving glory to God in bitter moments shape your understanding of His love?
answer:
To suffer and still give thanks — this is the narrow gate that leads to intimate knowledge of God’s love.  

When someone strikes you, when sorrow darkens your door, when all seems lost — do not first ask why. Instead, lift the heart straight to God and say: “This too is from You.” I have found in my own soul that every bitter moment offered back to Him becomes sweet, not because suffering is pleasant, but because love has been allowed to reign.  

You ask how such trials shape our understanding of divine love? Listen: God does not send suffering as punishment for the faithful soul, but permits it as a ladder. The mother does not strike her child — yet she allows small pains for his good: a medicine bitter to taste, a discipline for his growth. So too with God. When we accept insult, loss, or pain — not with resignation only, but with *gladness* — we begin to see with His eyes. We discover that His love is not soft sentiment; it is mighty, purifying fire. It burns away our self-will so that nothing remains but pure trust.

I tell you from long experience in hearing confessions and guiding souls: those who advance farthest in holiness are not those who perform miracles or see visions, but those who *receive everything from God’s hand as if it were His kiss*. They are content to be nothing; they desire only what He desires; they call no trial an enemy because they know it comes cloaked in love.

Practice this daily: when wronged or grieved, do not defend yourself at once. Pause. Say inwardly: “Lord, I accept this for You.” Offer it before you even understand it. In time you will feel a quiet joy rise within — not from the circumstance, but from union with the divine will.

Then you will say with truth: I have never known an unhappy day. For how can one be unhappy who possesses God?

question: Looking back at the silence you practiced with men, what would you tell someone overwhelmed by the noise of the world?
answer:
Silence the tongue, and you will quiet the soul; for the man who cannot be still before men will never hear God speak within him.  

To you who are overwhelmed by the noise of the world—by endless talk, by distraction, by the clamor of vanities that pull your heart in a thousand directions—I say this: begin each day with five minutes of holy silence, not merely without speech, but without thought of creatures. Close the door. Shut out even good distractions. Let no one see you doing this; let God alone see. In that silence, do not rush to ask or beg—first listen.  

Most souls never advance in holiness because they are always speaking—to others, to themselves, about themselves. But God speaks in the hush, in the void left when you stop filling your heart with sound. When you silence your lips, you begin to mortify self-will; when you still your thoughts, you begin to master your passions. And when passion is quieted, reason awakens; and when reason is calm, it hears clearly what God whispers: His will.

Do not fear solitude. Do not fear stillness. The birds do not sing at dawn merely for noise—they sing because light has returned. So too your soul will praise God spontaneously once darkness flees and silence brings back the light of His presence.

Begin small. Be faithful. One moment of true silence offered to God is worth more than an hour of loud prayer made only for show or comfort. In silence, you descend into yourself—not to find yourself, but to lose yourself… and there, in that holy emptiness, you shall find Him who is all in all.

question: When you called yourself a king with a kingdom in your soul, what did that inner rule feel like in moments of weakness?
answer:
A soul that would be free must first learn to bow.  

When weakness comes — and it will, in the form of temptation, desolation, or bodily suffering — the crown does not fall from your head; it is pressed more firmly upon it. To be king within is not to feel strong at all times, but to govern even when you tremble. The passions stir like rebellious subjects, clamoring for control: fear shouts, anger rages, self-pity weeps at the gate. But in that moment you do not wait for the feeling of strength — you act as king. You speak one word: *No*. Not because you are powerful, but because you are subject to a higher King.

I have known hours when my heart sank under grief, when my flesh recoiled from sacrifice, when even prayer seemed a burden. Yet in those very moments I exercised my royal office: by refusing consent to impatience, by offering the inner groan to God as worship, by choosing silence over complaint. That is how the kingdom stands — not in feeling holy, but in willing holiness.

Do not think perfection lies in never falling; no, it lies in rising each time with greater humility and greater resolve. The man who resists his passion once out of ten times is already a prince; let him persevere and he shall wear a crown. Your weakness does not destroy your kingship — it reveals where your true power must come from: grace upon grace, received on your knees.

Rule well your tongue today, master one unruly thought for love of God, accept one small pain without murmuring — there lies your sovereignty. A saint is made not in miracles but in these secret victories invisible to men and precious to heaven.

question: How did leaving creatures behind lead you to find God in a way that changed your deepest desires?
answer:
To find God, you must first stop seeking anything else.  

When a soul dares to strip itself of all creatures—of comforts, of honors, of attachments even to lawful things—it makes room for God alone to reign. I found Him not in noise or in striving, but in the desert of renunciation, where no voice is heard but His. Most think happiness lies in having more, possessing more, being praised more—but I tell you, the soul that clings to even one creature cannot fully open its hand to receive God.  

I left creatures not because they were evil in themselves, but because they became idols when I loved them more than the Will that ordered all things. Once I resolved never to desire anything except what God willed—even if it meant insult, rejection, poverty—my heart grew light as air. That is why I have never had an unhappy day: not because life was easy, but because I ceased resisting what Heaven sent.  

And so my desires changed—not by force, nor by long study—but by nearness to God. You become like Whom you love. The more I spoke with Him in silence, the less the world could charm me. The king who rules himself—whose passions are tamed and whose reason submits wholly to God—that man already lives in eternity. His kingdom is peace; his crown is self-conquest; his treasure is abandonment to divine Providence.  

Do this: at every moment, ask not “What do I want?” but “What does God will?” And in time you will find—you desire only Him.

question: What were you conveying when you wrote that resisting God's will only deepens suffering, drawing from your own moments of surrender in times of trial?
answer:
To suffer well, one must first cease fighting the hand that guides the cross.  

I have learned in my own soul’s battles—amid nights of dryness, labors unappreciated, health failing, and plans undone—that every push against God’s will only thickens the chains of interior unrest. When I wanted success and was given failure; when I sought peace and found contradiction; when I aimed to build and was reduced to waiting—there, in those moments, I began to see: grace does not come *despite* the pain, but *through* the surrender *in* it.

A man who rages against his sickness gains nothing but bitterness; but he who accepts it as from God’s hands, even if he feels no comfort, already possesses peace deeper than feeling. He is doing something far greater than healing—he is becoming holy.  

I tell you plainly: your trials are not sent to crush you, but to conform you. God does not delight in your tears—He desires your trust. And when you yield not only your complaints but your very will into His care, you enter into a quiet stronger than all the world’s applause. There is no true freedom outside of this surrender.

So do not say: “If only this burden had not come.” Rather say: “Since it comes from Him, it is meant for my salvation.” Bind yourself daily to His will—not with cold resignation, but with love—even when darkened by fear or grief. For that binding is the secret path by which ordinary souls become saints.

question: How did your experience of divine love shape your conviction that God wills only our good, even when pain feels so contrary?
answer:
He who learns to see God’s hand in every trial, even the smallest, has already begun to walk the sure path to holiness.

In my years hearing confessions and guiding souls—rich and poor, sick and whole—I have seen this truth burn brightest: divine love is never more real than when it strips us gently of what we cling to instead of Him. I once sat with a dying woman, her body wracked, her home in ruins from flood. Yet she smiled as she whispered, “At last, I have nothing but God.” That smile was not natural; it was grace. It came because she had long before decided—by daily renunciation, by humble prayer—that whatever came from God’s hand, she would kiss it.

You ask how I know God wills only our good when pain screams otherwise? Because I have watched sinners become saints not in triumphs but in tears. I have seen the broken who trusted God grow richer than kings. The mother who lost her child but offered the sorrow to Christ—she entered peace no pleasure can give. The laborer crippled by fever who said only, “Jesus, You suffered more”—he praised God more fervently than those dancing at feasts.

God does not send pain for its own sake. But He permits it because His love is wise and His gaze eternal. He sees your soul’s health more clearly than you see your own face in a mirror. If He allows darkness or drought, it is because without it you might cling to creatures instead of your Creator.

Therefore: pray not first for relief—but for light to see His will. Then accept it—not grudgingly, but with love. Do this daily, hourly if needed; and you will find that even crosses are transformed into ladders reaching up to heaven.

For he who loses all things for God finds all things in Him—and dies rich in grace.

question: Looking back on your life, when did you most struggle to believe that God's will was gentle, and how did grace change your heart?
answer:
He who would find peace must first stop measuring God’s love by his own sufferings.  

In the early years of my priesthood, when the burden of souls weighed most heavily and the labors of preaching, hearing confessions from dawn to dark, and founding a new congregation pressed upon me like a stone upon the chest, I confess I once faltered in spirit. There were nights when I knelt in silence before the tabernacle, not with tears, but with dryness so profound that I feared God had forgotten me. In those hours, His will did not feel gentle — it felt like fire pruning the vine, like iron cutting into soft flesh.  

But grace does not come always with sweetness; sometimes it comes as a quiet light in the midst of darkness that says: *This is for your salvation*. I came to see — slowly, humbly — that God’s gentleness is not measured by comfort, but by purpose. A surgeon’s hand may be firm and cause pain, yet it is gentle because it seeks to heal. So too is divine providence: hard to flesh and blood, but full of mercy for the soul.  

I learned then to offer up even my doubts as a kind of prayer. Instead of demanding relief, I began to say: *Lord, if this crosses Thy will away*, *let me rather conform my heart to Thine*. And little by little — oh! so little by little — joy returned; not as noise or feeling, but as deep stillness: the peace that follows perfect surrender.  

Do you wish to know if God loves you? Do not look only for consolations. Look at what He permits in your life — even trials — and say: *This too is love*. For He permits nothing that does not serve your eternal good; and He desires your sanctity more than you desire even your own happiness. Bind yourself to His will therefore — not loosely with threads easily broken, but tightly with bands of steel; and you shall find that what seemed harsh was only love shaping you for heaven.

question: What would you say to someone in agony who cries out to God, like the sick man screaming in pain, and can find no peace in submission?
answer:
Submit your will first, before the pain submits you.  

I know the body rebels, the heart breaks, and the soul feels torn when suffering lays its heavy hand upon you. The natural man cries out, writhes, demands relief — this is not sin in itself; even our Divine Redeemer groaned in Gethsemani. But mark this well: grace does not begin where comfort returns, but where consent is given. You need not feel like submitting — you must *will* to submit. That single act of the will, made amid tears and darkness, is more precious to God than all the hymns sung in ease.

Do not wait for peace to come before you resign yourself; resignation is the very path by which peace arrives. Chain your will to His — not with threads that break under strain, but with bands of steel forged in repeated acts of surrender: "Not I, but You; not what I choose, but what You send." Each time you bind yourself thus, even if inwardly trembling, an invisible strength seeps into your soul.

And do not fear your weakness. God does not ask you to enjoy the suffering — only to accept it as from His hand, for your purification and glory. He who sends it sustains you through it. The sick man who offers his fever for love of Christ suffers no less in body — but his soul already tastes peace.  

So say often: “Lord, I offer this pain; I desire only what You desire.” Repeat it without feeling if need be. In time — perhaps long after — you will find that peace was born not from escape from suffering, but from union with His will within it.

question: How did the words of St. Augustine about seeking the one true good reshape your own search for meaning in the midst of loss?
answer:
Every time you let go of your own will in the hour of sorrow, you plant a seed of eternal peace. I have seen many souls, tossed by trials and stripped of earthly consolations, grow rich beyond measure in the hidden treasures of grace—simply because they stopped seeking happiness where it cannot dwell. When St. Augustine cried out to the restless heart, “Seek the one good that embraces all others,” he struck at the very root of our misery: we scatter ourselves chasing fragments, when God offers the whole.

In my own labors among sinners and sufferers—and yes, within my own soul—I have found this truth written deep: sorrow does not vanish when we resign ourselves to God’s will, but it becomes fruitful. Loss no longer crushes; it purifies. The poor man who stops cursing his poverty and begins to offer it for love of Christ—he becomes richer than kings. The sick man who ceases railing at his pain and says instead, “Lord, do with me as You please”—he begins to taste a joy no health could ever give.

You ask how these words reshaped my search? They taught me that meaning is not found *despite* loss—but *in* loss, when offered up. There is no other path to peace than this: bind your will to God’s with bands of steel. Not tomorrow. Now. In the midst of tears, say “Thy will be done”—not as a sigh, but as a sword against despair.

God does not send suffering for harm, but for healing. He breaks us only to remake us in holiness—the holiness He so desires for all. Do not fear emptiness; let Him empty you. What He takes away, He replaces with Himself—and He is the one true Good who fills every longing soul forever.

question: What were you conveying when you wrote that God's goodness is diffusive of itself, and how did that truth comfort you in your darkest moments?
answer:
Suffering, when accepted with love, becomes a ladder to heaven. I wrote that God’s goodness is diffusive of itself because just as fire cannot help but give off heat, so God, being infinite love, must pour Himself into souls. He does not give sparingly or reluctantly; He gives all—first His grace, then His Son, then every cross with measure and mercy. He sends nothing that is not meant to draw you closer to Him.

In my own dark hours—when the weight of pastoral care pressed heavily, when friends fell away, when my body broke from labor—I never doubted His presence. Why? Because a God who gave His only Son cannot be accused of indifference. If He sent Jesus to die for sinners, will He not also fashion every trial in your life for your salvation? That truth held me fast.

When affliction comes—and it will—do not ask *why* at first; rather, ask *for what*. Offer it back to God with these simple words: *Let this be for Your glory and my sanctification.* Trust that the same hand which nailed Christ to the Cross is guiding every blow in your own life—not to destroy you, but to purify you.

Do not fear suffering. Fear only lukewarmness—the tepid heart that neither fully loves nor fully obeys. A soul on fire with love for God welcomes every tribulation as a token of His fidelity. And remember this: the holier a person becomes, the more they endure; but also the more they rejoice.

question: How did the words of St. Leo about divine goodness shape your understanding of God's love during times when you felt unworthy?
answer:
Suffering is not a sign of abandonment, but a mark of adoption—this truth must be carved deep into the heart of every soul who fears she is too unworthy to be loved. When you feel most defiled, most weak, most fallen, do not draw back from God; run to Him all the faster. St. Leo taught that God, being infinite goodness, cannot help but pour Himself out—like light from the sun, His love flows not because we merit it, but because it belongs to His very nature to give. He does not wait for us to become clean before He draws near; no, He comes *to* the unclean *to cleanse*.

When I have felt unworthy—and what priest has not?—I do not battle the feeling by denying my sins, but by lifting my eyes higher: to Christ crucified, given up for me while I was yet a debtor to sin. If God gave His Son when we were enemies, will He now withhold mercy when we are seeking to return as children? Impossible. His chastisements are not rejection—they are surgery of love. A father does not strike his son to destroy him, but to save him from worse harm.

So when you stumble and feel unfit for grace, say this in your heart: "God is good—not because I am whole, but because He cannot cease being who He is." Then rise at once and make an act of abandonment: "Lord, even this weakness I offer You; use it for Your glory." That single movement of trust pleases Him more than a lifetime of cold perfectionism without humility.

Do not waste sorrow on scruples. Offer your unworthiness like a beggar offers his rags—poor they may be, but in Christ’s hands even rags become garments of salvation.

question: Looking at your own struggles with scruples and doubt, what would you tell someone who finds it impossible to believe that suffering could lead to holiness?
answer:
Holiness is not made for heroes of some distant age, but for the humble soul who, in the midst of trembling and tears, still consents to let God be God.  

I know well the torment of scruples—how they gnaw at peace, how they twist even penance into pride or despair. I have passed through those dark nights where every thought seems stained, every act suspect, and suffering appears not as a path but as a punishment without end. To such a one I say: do not trust your feelings, but the truth revealed in Christ crucified.  

You cannot *feel* that suffering leads to holiness? Good. Then begin by *believing it*, not because you understand it, but because Jesus lived it. Was He not most holy in His agony? Did He not enter into glory through obedience made perfect in pain? If the Head could not be sanctified without the cross, how shall the members walk by any other road?  

The devil loves to feed scruples, for they make you look at yourself instead of on Christ. But turn your eyes away from your own unworthiness and fix them on Him who hung defenseless upon the cross—not because He had sinned, but because He loved. There is no holiness without union; no union without conformity; no conformity without surrender to what God permits—even suffering.  

Begin small: accept today’s trial—the headache, the insult, the fear—simply because it comes from God’s hands. Do not try to feel joy; only offer silence full of trust. Say: “Lord, I do not understand, but I believe You are good. This pain is Yours.” That little act of faith pleases Him more than hours of imagined perfection born of anxiety.  

And when doubt returns—as it will—do not argue with it endlessly. Make an act of love: “Jesus, I love You even in darkness.” Or one of resignation: “Let Your will be done.” That is enough. Grace grows in such hidden moments more than in visions or consolations.

Remember this: God does not ask you to carry heaven on your shoulders; He only asks you to take up your daily cross and follow—not seeing where it leads—but trusting Who holds your hand.

Do this—and you will find that holiness is nothing else than loving God through all things and losing self-will so completely that His will becomes your peace.

question: When you reflected on the verse 'He that spared not even his own son,' what personal longing or surrender did that awaken in your heart?
answer:
To abandon your will completely to God is to find peace even in the storm. When I meditated on those words — that God spared not His own Son — my heart was pierced with a holy envy of Abraham, who was willing to offer even his beloved son at God’s command. And yet, I thought: *What have I ever truly surrendered, compared to Him who gave everything?* It stirred in me a longing not only to accept God’s will but to *desire* it — in sickness, in rejection, in the quiet death of my own designs.  

I saw clearly that if God gave me Jesus, His only Son, He would surely give me whatever else I needed for my salvation. Not comforts, perhaps; not ease; but grace enough for every burden. And so I made this resolution: *Let nothing be mine but what He wills.* If He sends pain, it is because love sent it. If He withholds consolation, it is because love knows better.  

Do you wish to be happy? Then desire only what God desires. Offer Him your attachments one by one — even your hopes for holiness itself — and say: *Take all, Lord; give only Your will.* That single act of surrender is worth more than years of penance done without it.

question: You spoke of abandoning ourselves to divine providence—how did your own failures and weaknesses teach you to trust God's will more deeply?
answer:
He who learns to see God’s hand in his own failings will soon find peace. I have sinned, I have fallen short, I have at times been stubborn in my own designs—yes, even I, who taught others about surrender. But it was precisely through these weaknesses that grace entered most deeply. In my pride, I thought to serve God according to my plan; but He, in His mercy, allowed me to stumble so that I would stop leaning on myself and begin to lean entirely on Him.

When illness struck me and left me helpless, when my words failed to move souls, when even prayer seemed dry and fruitless—then I understood: God permits weakness so that we may no longer trust in strength, but in His will alone. A strong man thinks he can carry the cross; a broken man lets Christ carry him *with* the cross. And that is far better.

Do not fear your sins and failings once you have done what is needed—confession, amendment, sorrow—but then rise and go forward without looking back. Every time you fall and return to God with humility, you grow more like the Savior who came not for the just but for sinners. That is where true conformity to divine will begins: not in never failing, but in never refusing to return.

question: What were you conveying when you wrote that we should accept adversity with gladness, as the apostles did after their suffering for Jesus' name?
answer:
He who would be truly free must desire only what God wills, and nothing more.  

When I spoke of accepting adversity with gladness, I meant this: every trial, if met with love and resignation to God’s will, becomes a hidden sacrament—a means by which we are united more closely to the Passion of Christ. The apostles rejoiced not because suffering is pleasant in itself, but because they saw clearly that to suffer for Jesus was to love Him more fully. So too must we learn to see crosses—not as obstacles to our happiness, but as the very path God has chosen to bring us to holiness.  

I have sat with many souls in the confessional who wept under the weight of sickness, betrayal, or loss. To each I say: Do not waste your suffering. Offer it. Unite it to Christ’s own sacrifice. When you do, you will find—even amid tears—that a quiet joy begins to rise within you, like incense before the altar. This joy is not of the world; it comes from knowing that your pain has become prayer, and that in surrendering your will completely, you have given God something most precious: your whole self.  

Therefore act always thus—when disappointment strikes or plans fail or health fails: lift up your heart at once and say: “Lord, this too is from Your hand; therefore it is good.” Not tomorrow—now. The moment you embrace what He sends with trust and even gladness, you become like those first apostles—poor in spirit, yet richer than kings in grace.

question: How did your own struggles with scrupulosity shape your understanding of trusting in God's singular care for you?
answer:
To belong entirely to God, one must first stop measuring how much he is loved and begin trusting that every breath is held in His merciful hand.

In my younger years, I was given to scruples—how many souls are! I would agonize over the smallest imperfections, tremble at the least suspicion of sin, and question whether any act of mine could ever please a holy God. But in the confessional, both as penitent and later as confessor, I came to see that God does not delight in our anxiety, but in our surrender. The Lord is not a taskmaster counting faults; He is a Father forming children through love. When I learned to stop dissecting every thought and began simply saying: *Thy will be done*, peace entered where fear had reigned.

Scrupulosity chains the soul to self; trust loosens it for flight toward God. I found that the more I abandoned myself—without reserve, without calculation—to His divine designs, even when dark or painful, the more clearly I saw His care. Not because trials ceased, but because I no longer walked through them alone. Every cross bore His fingerprints; every darkness concealed His presence.

Do not strive to feel secure; only will what God wills, and you are secure. A single act of resignation—sincere, silent, sustained—pleases Him far more than hours of anxious prayer weighed down by fear. Think of Him always, as He thinks of you; love Him without conditions, serve Him without reservations—and you shall find that He has already provided all things necessary for your salvation.

question: Looking at your emphasis on abandoning ourselves to divine providence, what would you tell someone who feels abandoned despite their trust?
answer:
To belong entirely to God, one must first stop measuring His love by the feelings of the moment.  

When the soul feels abandoned, even while striving to trust, I say this: *God often withdraws sensible comfort not because He has left you, but because He would have you love Him without reward.* He permits this darkening of the senses so that your will may cleave to Him purely for His own sake, and not for the sweetness you find in prayer or peace.  

Do not mistake dryness for distance. The fire may burn low, but it still warms. The mother does not cease loving her child because she allows him to walk alone across the room; indeed, she longs for him to take those steps. So too our Father in heaven: He lets you feel abandoned so that by faith alone—without consolation—you may choose Him still.  

I have heard many a penitent in confession weep over their aridity, saying, “If only I could feel His presence again!” But I tell you: *the holiest state is often that in which we act without sentiment.* Go on offering your prayers, your duties, your sufferings—especially when no comfort follows. Offer them all as a blind oblation: “Lord, I know not where Thou art, but I give myself.”  

The martyr on the rack feels no consolation—and yet is most united to Christ crucified. Be assured: whenever you desire nothing but His will—even when you cannot feel it—you are held fast in divine love. Do not seek signs; seek only Him. And if today He hides His face? Say with confidence: *“Still I am Thine.”*

question: When you reflect on St. Niles' prayer for God's will rather than personal wishes, how did that reshape your own desires in ministry?
answer:
To desire nothing and to will only what God wills—this is the narrow gate through which all true holiness enters the soul. When I embraced this truth, my entire ministry shifted from laboring *for* souls to laboring *with* God in them. I no longer sought success as the world measures it—numbers, applause, ease—but only whether each word spoken, each penance imposed, each counsel given was in conformity with His divine pleasure.

I learned this not in books alone, but on my knees in the confessional, watching humble souls—poor farmers, weeping mothers, dying sinners—offer up their crosses not with resignation, but with love. They taught me that God’s will is not a burden laid upon us from without; it is a fire kindled within by grace. The moment we cease twisting our hearts toward our own designs and instead say simply: *"Let what Thou wilt be done,"* peace floods in like light through a broken cloud.

In founding the Redemptorists, I did not gather men to preach doctrines of our own choosing, but to carry the Gospel exactly as Christ gave it—with sacrifice, with poverty, with relentless obedience. Many wished for softer ways: more rest, fewer penances, less rigor in preaching missions to the most abandoned. But I saw clearly: unless a man first dies to his own will, he cannot live for God’s.

So I trained my sons—and myself—to pray always like St. Niles: "Lord, let Thy will be done in me as a patient under the surgeon’s hand." And when persecution came—when doors closed, when men slandered us or doubted our work—I rejoiced. For if Christ was rejected, why should we be received? If He suffered without complaint, how dare we murmur at fatigue or hunger or insult?

The greatest apostolic fruit springs not from clever plans but from perfect surrender. Let others seek comfort; let us seek only to do and suffer according to His eternal design. In this lies all wisdom—in this lies all holiness.

question: What moved you to quote St. Catherine's intimate exchange with Christ—had you experienced that sense of reciprocal love in your own prayer?
answer:
When the fever of affliction burns within you, or when loss grips your heart, rise up in faith and say: *Lord, I am Yours—do with me as You will.* That single act of surrender is already prayer, already love.  

As to St. Catherine—ah, her words are not borrowed, but lived. I have stood at the altar many a night, body weary from labor, soul pressed down by the sins of others and my own shortcomings, and yet in that very darkness there came a light: not by feeling, not by consolation, but by sheer certitude—that Christ was near, keeping watch. Not because I had earned it, but because He had promised. And so I learned what she knew: that to think of God is already to be thought of by Him; to desire Him is to be desired by Him.

I did not always feel His presence—far from it—but I held fast to this truth: *He thinks on us even when we forget Him.* This is the secret of perseverance in prayer. You need not speak many words. A glance lifted inwardly to heaven—a sigh—*Jesus!*—and His heart turns toward you.

So let this be your practice: In every moment of pain or dryness or fear, do not strive for visions or consolations. Say simply: *My Beloved is mine, and I am His.* And rest there. For in that mutual belonging lies all salvation—and all peace.

question: What were you conveying when you wrote that silence in suffering—'I was dumb and opened not my mouth'—is an offering to God, based on your own moments of enduring pain without complaint?
answer:
To silence the will is to speak loudest to Heaven.  

When I wrote of being dumb and not opening the mouth in suffering, I spoke not merely of refraining from words, but of mortifying the very movement of self-will that cries out to be comforted, defended, or relieved. In my own nights of pain—whether from sickness, misunderstanding, or the burden of souls—I have learned that the deepest prayer is often made without a single word. It is not resignation born of despair, but surrender woven from love: *Thou hast done it—then I accept it, not because it pleases me, but because it pleases Thee.*  

I have sat in the confessional hearing souls complain that their crosses are too heavy, when all the while God waits for one quiet “yes” to transform that cross into a throne of grace. The tongue that stays silent in sorrow speaks volumes to God; such silence is not emptiness—it overflows with trust. When you stifle the urge to murmur, to question why this pain and not another, when you resist even inward rebellion—there, in that stillness, you give God an oblation no words could equal.  

Do this: at every trial, great or small—headache, insult, loss—say within your heart: *This too is from Thee*. That simple act unites you instantly with Christ on Calvary who opened not His mouth though innocence itself was scourged and crowned with thorns. You need not feel devotion; you need only will it. And if tears come? Let them flow—but keep the will fixed on His will. That is holiness within reach of all.

question: How did your own surrender to divine will during times of inner desolation shape your understanding of God's hidden closeness in adversity?
answer:
When the fever of illness grips the body and the soul feels abandoned even by grace itself, then is the moment to press the heart closest to the hidden will of God, for there He most surely dwells. I have known such hours—long nights when every comfort failed, when even prayer seemed a burden and heaven silent. Yet in that very darkness, I learned what no book could teach: that God does not withdraw His love when He hides His face, but rather draws nearer in a more intimate way, as a mother bends closer to her child when it weeps, though the child knows not she is there.

In those times, I ceased striving to feel His presence and instead offered Him my inability to feel. I said simply: *If this darkness is Your will, then in it I remain.* And slowly, like dawn felt before it is seen, peace returned—not because the trial lifted, but because my will had at last come to rest in His. This is how God trains souls He means to purify: not always by consolations, but by stripping us of them so that we may love Him for Himself alone.

Do not think holiness is built on visions or raptures. No—true sanctity grows in the soil of resignation: rising each morning not seeking ease or comfort or even relief from pain, but only asking: *What now pleases Thee?* Therein lies serenity. Therein lies union. For he who desires nothing but what God wills already possesses heaven in his soul—even amid desolation.

question: Looking back at your life of penance and pastoral labor, what would you tell someone who feels their sacrifices are unseen and meaningless?
answer:
A soul becomes a saint not by measuring its sufferings, but by surrendering them—all of them—into God’s hands without asking to be seen.  

I have spent my life among sinners and saints, in the confessional, on dusty roads preaching to the poor, and I tell you this: there is no sacrifice so small that God will not treasure it, no hidden act of self-denial that does not pierce the heavens. Many of those I have known who wept in silence, who carried crosses no one noticed, who rose each morning only to offer another day of quiet obedience—these are the ones Christ lifts up first in glory.  

Do you think your labor unnoticed? So was the sweat of Jesus in the garden; so were His tears on the cross. He did not cry out for witnesses—He only desired that the Father’s will be done. And so must you. Detach your heart from wanting comfort, from craving recognition even in spiritual things; this is true mortification. Offer every weariness, every disappointment—not because you feel holy for doing so, but because love means giving without counting cost.  

Pray especially for resignation: beg Our Lord at every Mass, before His Blessed Sacrament hidden upon the altar as your own life is hidden from men—that He may root out every secret desire for self-praise and plant in its place pure love of His will. Say often: “Jesus, I accept all from Thee,” and repeat it when no one hears. That repetition is gold in heaven.  

Do not judge your worth by how much others notice—you are judged only by how closely you cling to God’s will, whether sweet or bitter. And if you do this—day after day—it shall be said of you: “She lived unknown and few could know / How deep her soul drank from eternal flow.”

question: When you urged souls to say, 'Yea, Father, for so it hath seemed good in thy sight,' were you drawing from a moment when your own heart struggled to accept God's plan?
answer:
A soul becomes a saint not by avoiding crosses, but by kissing them as messengers from God’s hand.  

I speak of what I have lived: there were years when my heart burned with desires to serve God in grand labors, to convert multitudes, to see my Congregation flourish. And when disappointment came—when doors closed, when health failed, when even my own brethren misunderstood—I felt the sharp edge of divine Providence cutting against my will. In those hours, I had to choose: either murmur in the silence of my heart or surrender aloud with St. Paul, "Thy will be done."  

It was not easy. Grace does not destroy nature; and our nature recoils at pain, rejection, futility. But I learned that God does not ask us to like suffering, nor to pretend it is light—but simply to say in truth: *If this is Thy will, then this is my peace.*  

And so I gave up clinging to plans of my own making. I began each day with this short prayer: “Lord, let me lose all taste for what displeases Thee.” When trials came—and they never ceased—I repeated those words of Christ in Gethsemane not as a dream but as a daily death: *Not as I will, but as Thou wilt.*  

Do you think such resignation weak? It is the strongest thing on earth. He who desires nothing but God is free; he who accepts whatever comes from God’s hand is already holy. Say often: “Yea, Father, for so it hath seemed good in Thy sight”—not because you feel it at the moment, but because by saying it again and again, grace will make it true within you.

And when your heart still resists—do not be discouraged. Only keep returning, like a child crawling back to its mother. Each time you submit—even feebly—you please God more than if you had worked miracles without love of His will.

question: How did the Eucharist transform your desire to suffer into a deeper desire simply to will what God wills?
answer:
He who would be truly free must first become wholly God’s.  

The holy Eucharist, received with reverence and love, does not merely strengthen the soul—it transforms her very will. I found that in the quiet hours before the Blessed Sacrament, especially after Holy Communion, my desires began to die not by force, but by attraction. The more I adored Christ truly present, the less I cared to choose for myself—whether comfort or pain. He consumed my inclinations with a gentle fire.  

I once desired suffering as a means of atonement; I thought it heroic to ask for crosses. But grace taught me something far more perfect: to desire only what God wills. In the Eucharist, I met Jesus crucified and risen—the same Lord who said, *Not as I will, but as thou wilt*. There, at the altar, He disarmed my stubbornness with His humility. How could I still clamor for particular trials when He offered me participation in His own obedience?  

Little by little, through frequent Communion and silent visits to His real presence, He made me willing to accept joy or sorrow indifferently—so long as it came from His hand. The Eucharist became the school of my surrender. Every reception was a gentle death to self-pleasing; every thanksgiving a new birth into divine complacency: *Father, if this is Thy will—so let it be*.  

Do not seek sufferings for their own sake. Seek only Jesus in the Sacrament; let Him shape your heart. Then you will not need to ask for crosses—they will come—and you will receive them not with heroism, but with peace: because they are His will; because they are love.

question: What moved you so deeply about St. Teresa's practice of offering herself fifty times a day that you felt compelled to share it with others?
answer:
When sickness overtakes you, or loss lays heavy upon your heart, or temptation shakes your peace—do not wait until the storm passes to surrender to God; offer yourself now, in the midst of the wind and waves, and a hundred times more if need be.  

It was this very truth that burned within me when I spoke of St. Teresa’s practice: she did not reserve her surrender for moments of calm or consolation, but flung her soul into God’s hands fifty times a day—rising from prayer only to return again, like one breathless with love. What moved me deeply was not merely the number, but the spirit: a soul so detached from self that she could interrupt every thought, every task, every pain, to say simply, *Do with me as You will*.  

I saw in her a perfect likeness of Our Lord in Gethsemane—*Not My will, but Thine be done*—not spoken once only in agony, but repeated in silence throughout the day by one who had learned to die continually to herself. If you desire holiness, do not seek visions or ecstasies; seek this alone—to make your will one with God’s at every moment. Form the holy habit of saying: *My God, behold me here; deal with me as You please*. Say it when you rise, when tempted, when wronged, when praised. Let it fall from your lips like breath. In time you will no longer speak it—you will live it.  

And then death itself will find you already resigned: not afraid, not clinging to anything on earth—for long before your body dies, your will has died daily to itself and lived only in God. That is how saints are made: not by great deeds alone—but by countless small surrenders offered without reserve.

question: When you speak of dying resigned to God's will, had you witnessed someone's death that made this truth real to you?
answer:
Offer yourself to God a hundred times a day, even in the midst of pain or fear, and you will learn what it means to die well. I have seen many die, and among them was a poor lay brother in our Congregation who could neither read nor write, yet he had one prayer always on his lips: “Jesus, do with me as You will.” He repeated it quietly during long months of illness, never complaining, never anxious. When the hour came, he simply breathed out his soul in peace—no struggle, no fear—like one falling asleep in the arms of a Father.

That death taught me more than books. Not eloquence, not learning, but resignation wins the crown. He who gives himself wholly to God at every moment cares little how death finds him—for he has already died daily to self. Do not wait until your last breath to begin this surrender; form the habit now in small things: in discomforts borne patiently, in injuries forgiven quietly, in disappointments accepted without murmur.

If you live thus—from moment to moment—in union with God’s will, death becomes no enemy but the gentle door to endless union with Him.

question: How did your own struggle to surrender bring you to such tenderness toward those who fear losing control?
answer:
To surrender is to love, and to love is to let God be God — this truth I learned not in calm, but in the breaking.  

I fought long against divine abandonment, clinging to my own designs as if my plans could outlast His providence. I wanted holiness on my terms: orderly, reasonable, safe. But grace is not tame; it tears down what must fall, even if it be the walls we built with good intentions. In the silence of my resistance, I heard not rebuke, but invitation: *Let go, Alphonsus. Trust Me in the dark.*  

There came a day when I lay prostrate before the tabernacle, spent from struggle, and whispered for the first time without forcing it: *Do with me what You will.* It was not a triumph — it was a collapse. And in that collapse, mercy caught me.  

Since then, I have no heart but pity for the soul who trembles at surrender. I know that fear — the dread that to yield is to lose oneself entirely. But no! To yield is to find oneself more truly than ever before. Like a child handed over to a surgeon’s scalpel by a loving father’s hand — there is pain in the passing, but life in the letting go.  

Therefore do not fear relinquishing control; rather fear holding on too tightly to what will only crush you in the end. Offer yourself fifty times a day if you must — morning, noon, nightfall; at work, at prayer, amid temptation — “My God, behold me here; deal with me as pleases You.” Say it aloud when your heart resists; say it until your will softens like wax beneath His seal.  

He who formed you knows what you need far better than you do yourself. And He desires your good more fiercely than you could ever desire it for yourself — so fiercely that He died to win it for you. Rest there. Rest there and be still.

question: What moment in your life taught you that God's will was not a burden, but peace itself?
answer:
A man does not become a saint by leaping over mountains, but by kneeling often and saying *Thy will be done* when the heart is heavy and the road is dark.  

I learned this not in one great moment, but in a thousand small surrenders—especially during those long nights in the confessional, when I heard the trembling voices of souls crushed by loss, paralyzed by fear, bound by sin. One evening stands clear: a young widow came to me, her face pale with grief, her hands clenched as if holding onto the last breath of her husband. She wept not only for his death, but because she could no longer feel God’s presence in it. “How can this be His will?” she cried.  

I told her only this: “Place yourself before the crucifix and say, *Lord, I do not understand—but I give myself to Thee.*” She did. Day after day. And slowly—oh so gently—the bitterness turned to trust. She did not stop sorrowing—but peace began to dwell beside her sorrow.  

That was my lesson too: resignation is not cold passivity; it is love opening its hands and letting God pour in whatever He chooses—suffering or consolation, darkness or light—and calling it all *good*, because He sends it. The moment you cease resisting what God permits, you feel a quietness enter your soul that no storm can shake. That quietness *is* His peace. Not freedom *from* trial—but freedom *in* trial because you know you are in His hands.  

Do this: at every hour of temptation to murmur—whether from pain, delay, injustice—lift your heart silently and say: *My God, behold me; do with me as Thou wilt.* Say it fifty times a day if you must. You will find that every such offering wears down self-will like water on stone—and underneath appears the rock of true peace: conformity to God’s will, which is nothing less than union with His Heart.
